narrative:
MEGILLUS, a Lacedaemonian.

"Tell me, Strangers, is a God or some man supposed to be the author of your laws?"

"A God, Stranger; in very truth a God," replied Cleinias. "Among us Cretans he is said to have been Zeus, but in Lacedaemon, whence our friend here comes, I believe they would say that Apollo is their lawgiver. Would they not, Megillus?"

"Certainly," said Megillus.

"And do you, Cleinias, believe, as Homer tells, that every ninth year Minos went to converse with his Olympian sire, and was inspired by him to make laws for your cities?"

"Yes, that is our tradition," replied Cleinias, "and there was Rhadamanthus, a brother of his, with whose name you are familiar; he is reputed to have been the justest of men, and we Cretans are of opinion that he earned this reputation from his righteous administration of justice when he was alive."
refashioned:
"Tell me, strangers -- is a god or a human being supposed to be the author of your laws?"

"A god, stranger -- truly a god," Cleinias answered. "Among us Cretans, it is said to have been Zeus. But in Sparta, where our friend here comes from, I believe they would say Apollo is their lawgiver. Isn't that right, Megillus?"

"Certainly," Megillus agreed.

"And do you, Cleinias, believe what Homer tells us -- that every nine years Minos went to converse with his father Zeus on Olympus, and was inspired by him to create laws for your cities?"

"Yes, that is our tradition," said Cleinias. "And there was Rhadamanthus, his brother, whose name you surely know. He is considered to have been the most just of men, and we Cretans believe he earned that reputation through his righteous administration of justice during his lifetime."
elegant:
"Tell me -- is a god or a man the author of your laws?"

"A god," Cleinias replied. "For Cretans it was Zeus; for the Spartans, Apollo."

The Stranger asked whether Cleinias believed Homer's account that Minos visited Zeus every nine years to receive divine instruction for lawmaking. Cleinias confirmed the tradition, adding that Minos's brother Rhadamanthus was renowned as the most just of men.
summary: The dialogue opens with the Athenian Stranger asking whether Cretan and Spartan laws have divine or human origins. Cleinias attributes Cretan law to Zeus and Spartan law to Apollo, and affirms the tradition that Minos received laws directly from Zeus every nine years.
distilled: The three interlocutors establish that both Cretan and Spartan legal systems claim divine authorship -- Zeus and Apollo respectively.
notes: Speakers: The Athenian Stranger opens with the question; Cleinias (Cretan) and Megillus (Spartan) respond. -- The dialogue begins in medias res, a common Platonic technique (cf. the opening of the Republic). -- Minos's visits to Zeus every nine years come from Odyssey 19.178-179. -- Rhadamanthus appears in Homer as a judge of the dead (Odyssey 4.564). -- The claim of divine lawgiving sets up the central tension: if laws are divinely authored, can they be criticized? The Stranger will challenge this assumption throughout. -- This opening parallels the Minos dialogue, also attributed to Plato, which examines the nature of law.

narrative: "Yes, and a noble reputation it was, worthy of a son of Zeus," said the Athenian Stranger. "As you and Megillus have been trained in these institutions, I dare say that you will not be unwilling to give an account of your government and laws; on our way we can pass the time pleasantly in talking about them, for I am told that the distance from Cnosus to the cave and temple of Zeus is considerable; and doubtless there are shady places under the lofty trees, which will protect us from this scorching sun. Being no longer young, we may often stop to rest beneath them, and get over the whole journey without difficulty, beguiling the time by conversation."
refashioned: "Yes, and a noble reputation it was -- worthy of a son of Zeus," the Stranger said. "Since you and Megillus have been raised under these institutions, I expect you won't mind giving an account of your government and laws. We can pass the time pleasantly discussing them on our walk, because I'm told the distance from Knossos to the cave and temple of Zeus is considerable. There must be shady spots under the tall trees to protect us from this scorching sun. Since we're no longer young, we can stop often to rest beneath them, and make the whole journey without difficulty, filling the time with conversation."
elegant: The Stranger proposes that the three old men discuss law and government during their long walk from Knossos to the cave of Zeus, resting in the shade along the way -- turning a physical journey into a philosophical one.
summary: The Athenian Stranger proposes they discuss government and laws during their walk from Knossos to the cave of Zeus, noting that the journey is long and the shade will provide comfortable resting places for men of their age.
distilled: The Stranger proposes a philosophical conversation about law to accompany their pilgrimage from Knossos to the cave of Zeus.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The cave of Zeus on Mount Ida (or Mount Dicte) was a famous Cretan sanctuary where Zeus was said to have been born and raised. -- The dramatic setting -- a long walk in scorching heat with rest stops -- parallels the Phaedrus, where Socrates and Phaedrus walk outside Athens and rest under a plane tree. -- The phrase 'beguiling the time by conversation' establishes the leisurely, exploratory tone of the Laws, which is the longest of Plato's dialogues. -- The three speakers are all elderly, which matters: the Laws represents Plato's final work, written in old age.

narrative:
"Yes, Stranger," said Cleinias, "and if we proceed onward we shall come to groves of cypresses, which are of rare height and beauty, and there are green meadows, in which we may repose and converse."

"Very good."

"Very good, indeed; and still better when we see them. Let us move on cheerily."

"I am willing," said the Athenian Stranger. "And first, I want to know why the law has ordained that you shall have common meals and gymnastic exercises, and wear arms."
refashioned:
"Yes, Stranger," said Cleinias, "and if we keep going we'll come to groves of cypresses -- remarkably tall and beautiful -- with green meadows where we can rest and talk."

"Excellent."

"Even better once we actually see them. Let's walk on with good spirits."

"Gladly," said the Stranger. "And here is my first question: why has your law ordained that you must have common meals, gymnastic exercises, and bear arms?"
elegant: Cleinias describes the beautiful cypress groves ahead. The Stranger agrees to walk on, then immediately poses his first substantive question: why does Cretan law mandate communal dining, gymnastics, and bearing arms?
summary: Cleinias describes the scenic route ahead with cypress groves and meadows. The Stranger agrees to continue walking and immediately raises his first question: why does Cretan law require common meals, gymnastics, and the bearing of arms?
distilled: The walk begins, and the Stranger poses his opening question: why does Cretan law mandate communal meals, exercise, and weaponry?
notes: Speakers: Cleinias begins, the Athenian Stranger concludes with the first substantive question. -- The cypress groves and meadows are typical of Cretan landscape along pilgrimage routes. -- The three institutions named -- syssitia (common meals), gymnasia, and arms-bearing -- are the pillars of both Cretan and Spartan military culture. -- The syssitia were communal dining arrangements central to Spartan and Cretan civic life; Aristotle discusses them in Politics 2.10. -- This question initiates the first major argument of Book 1: whether laws should be designed primarily for war.

narrative:
"I think, Stranger, that the aim of our institutions is easily intelligible to any one," replied Cleinias. "Look at the character of our country: Crete is not like Thessaly, a large plain; and for this reason they have horsemen in Thessaly, and we have runners -- the inequality of the ground in our country is more adapted to locomotion on foot; but then, if you have runners you must have light arms -- no one can carry a heavy weight when running, and bows and arrows are convenient because they are light.

"Now all these regulations have been made with a view to war, and the legislator appears to me to have looked to this in all his arrangements -- the common meals, if I am not mistaken, were instituted by him for a similar reason, because he saw that while they are in the field the citizens are by the nature of the case compelled to take their meals together for the sake of mutual protection. He seems to me to have thought the world foolish in not understanding that all are always at war with one another; and if in war there ought to be common meals and certain persons regularly appointed under others to protect an army, they should be continued in peace.

"For what men in general term peace would be said by him to be only a name; in reality every city is in a natural state of war with every other, not indeed proclaimed by heralds, but everlasting. And if you look closely, you will find that this was the intention of the Cretan legislator; all institutions, private as well as public, were arranged by him with a view to war; in giving them he was under the impression that no possessions or institutions are of any value to him who is defeated in battle; for all the good things of the conquered pass into the hands of the conquerors."
refashioned:
"I think the aim of our institutions is easily understood by anyone," Cleinias replied. "Consider the character of our land: Crete is not like Thessaly, a broad plain. That's why Thessaly has cavalry, while we have foot-soldiers. Our rough, hilly terrain is better suited to movement on foot. And if you have runners, you need light weapons -- no one can carry heavy equipment while running, so bows and arrows are practical because of their weight.

"All these arrangements were made with war in mind, and the lawgiver seems to have had this purpose behind everything he established. The common meals, I believe, were instituted for the same reason: he saw that soldiers in the field are naturally compelled to eat together for mutual protection, and he thought the world was foolish for not recognizing that everyone is always at war with everyone else. If common meals and organized command structures are necessary in wartime, they should be maintained in peacetime too.

"What most people call 'peace' he would say is just a word. In reality, every city exists in a permanent state of war with every other city -- not war declared by heralds, but an everlasting condition. Look closely and you'll find this was the Cretan lawgiver's guiding principle: every institution, private and public alike, was designed with war in view. His conviction was that no possessions or institutions have any value to the defeated, because everything the conquered own passes into the hands of the conquerors."
elegant: Cleinias explains that Crete's mountainous terrain shaped its military culture: light infantry instead of cavalry, bows instead of heavy arms. Every institution -- common meals, gymnastics, arms-bearing -- was designed for war. The lawgiver believed that what people call 'peace' is merely a name: in reality, every city is in a permanent state of war with every other. Nothing has value to the defeated, since all good things pass to the victors.
summary: Cleinias argues that all Cretan institutions were designed with war in mind, from light armaments suited to mountainous terrain to communal meals modeled on military camps. He presents the Cretan lawgiver's radical thesis: peace is an illusion, and every city exists in perpetual war with every other.
distilled: Cleinias articulates the Cretan lawgiver's foundational thesis: all institutions exist for war, because peace between cities is merely a name for undeclared perpetual conflict.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- This passage presents a Hobbesian 'war of all against all' centuries before Hobbes. -- The geographic determinism argument (terrain shapes military organization and thus political institutions) anticipates Montesquieu's climate theory in The Spirit of the Laws. -- The syssitia (common meals) were attributed to Minos by ancient tradition. Aristotle (Politics 2.10) notes that Cretan common meals predated Spartan ones. -- The claim that 'peace is only a name' echoes Heraclitus's fragment that 'war is the father of all things' (DK 53). -- The Stranger will spend much of Book 1 dismantling this war-centric view of legislation.

narrative: "You appear to me, Stranger, to have been thoroughly trained in the Cretan institutions, and to be well informed about them," said the Athenian Stranger. "Will you tell me a little more explicitly what is the principle of government which you would lay down? You seem to imagine that a well governed state ought to be so ordered as to conquer all other states in war: am I right in supposing this to be your meaning?"
refashioned: "You seem to have been thoroughly educated in Cretan institutions and to know them well," the Stranger said. "But tell me more precisely: what principle of government would you establish? You appear to believe that a well-governed state should be organized so as to conquer all other states in war. Am I correct in understanding your position?"
elegant: The Stranger distills Cleinias's position to a sharp question: does good governance reduce to military superiority over other states?
summary: The Athenian Stranger presses Cleinias to clarify his principle of government, asking whether he truly believes a well-governed state should be organized primarily to conquer other states in war.
distilled: The Stranger challenges Cleinias to confirm whether he equates good governance with military supremacy.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is the Socratic method at work: the Stranger restates Cleinias's position in its starkest form to test whether he will defend it. -- The question sets up the central argument of Book 1: whether virtue in its entirety, not just military courage, should be the aim of legislation. -- Cf. Republic 1, where Thrasymachus defines justice as the advantage of the stronger -- a similar reductive claim that Socrates dismantles.

narrative:
"Certainly; and our Lacedaemonian friend, if I am not mistaken, will agree with me," said Cleinias.

"Why, my good friend, how could any Lacedaemonian say anything else?" replied Megillus.

"And is what you say applicable only to states, or also to villages?" asked the Stranger.

"To both alike."

"The case is the same?"

"Yes."

"And in the village will there be the same war of family against family, and of individual against individual?"

"The same."

"And should each man conceive himself to be his own enemy -- what shall we say?"

"O Athenian Stranger -- inhabitant of Attica I will not call you, for you seem to deserve rather to be named after the goddess herself -- because you go back to first principles you have thrown a light upon the argument, and will now be better able to understand what I was just saying: that all men are publicly one another's enemies, and each man privately his own."

"My good sir, what do you mean?" asked the Athenian.

"Moreover, there is a victory and defeat -- the first and best of victories, the lowest and worst of defeats -- which each man gains or sustains at the hands, not of another, but of himself; this shows that there is a war against ourselves going on within every one of us."

"Let us now reverse the order of the argument," said the Stranger. "Seeing that every individual is either his own superior or his own inferior, may we say that there is the same principle in the house, the village, and the state?"

"You mean that in each of them there is a principle of superiority or inferiority to self?"

"Yes."

"You are quite right in asking the question, for there certainly is such a principle, and above all in states; and the state in which the better citizens win a victory over the mob and over the inferior classes may be truly said to be better than itself, and may be justly praised, where such a victory is gained, or censured in the opposite case."
refashioned:
"Certainly -- and our Spartan friend, unless I'm mistaken, will agree with me," said Cleinias.

"How could any Spartan say otherwise?" Megillus replied.

"Does your principle apply only to states, or also to villages?" the Stranger asked.

"To both equally."

"The situation is the same?"

"Yes."

"And within the village, there is the same war of family against family, individual against individual?"

"The same."

"And should each person consider himself his own enemy? What do we say to that?"

"Athenian Stranger -- no, I won't merely call you an inhabitant of Attica, for you deserve to be named after the goddess Athena herself -- by going back to first principles you've illuminated the argument. Now you'll better understand what I was saying: all people are publicly one another's enemies, and each person is privately his own enemy."

"My good sir, what exactly do you mean?"

"There is a victory and a defeat -- the first and best of victories, the lowest and worst of defeats -- that each person wins or suffers not at another's hands, but at his own. This proves there is a war within each of us against ourselves."

"Let's reverse the direction of the argument," said the Stranger. "If every individual is either superior or inferior to himself, can we say the same principle operates in the household, the village, and the state?"

"You mean that in each of them there is a principle of being superior or inferior to oneself?"

"Exactly."

"You're absolutely right to ask, because there certainly is such a principle -- above all in states. A state where the better citizens prevail over the mob and the inferior classes can truly be called better than itself and deserves praise. Where the opposite occurs, it deserves censure."
elegant: Megillus and Cleinias both affirm that war is the natural condition -- between states, villages, families, and individuals. The Stranger pushes the logic to its conclusion: each person is his own enemy. Cleinias embraces this, declaring the greatest victory is over oneself. The Stranger then reverses the argument: if individuals can be superior or inferior to themselves, the same applies to households, villages, and states. A state where better citizens prevail over worse ones is 'better than itself.'
summary: The Stranger extends the war thesis from states down to villages, families, and individuals, establishing that each person is his own enemy. Cleinias agrees that self-conquest is the highest victory. They conclude that a state where better citizens prevail over worse ones can be called 'superior to itself.'
distilled: The logic of universal war leads to its most important battlefield: the war within the self, where the greatest victory is self-mastery.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Megillus, and the Athenian Stranger in rapid exchange. -- Cleinias's compliment comparing the Stranger to Athena (goddess of wisdom) rather than merely calling him 'Athenian' is a mark of high respect. -- 'Superior to oneself' and 'inferior to oneself' are key Platonic concepts from Republic 4.430e-431a, where Socrates discusses self-mastery in relation to the tripartite soul. -- The idea that the greatest victory is over oneself appears also in Plato's Phaedrus (256b) and the Republic. -- The political extension -- a state 'better than itself' when the virtuous prevail -- anticipates the class analysis of the Republic. -- The Stranger is using the Socratic technique of scaling arguments: from individual to household to village to state (cf. Republic 2.368d-369a).

narrative: "Whether the better is ever really conquered by the worse, is a question which requires more discussion, and may be therefore left for the present," said the Athenian Stranger. "But I now quite understand your meaning when you say that citizens who are of the same race and live in the same cities may unjustly conspire, and having the superiority in numbers may overcome and enslave the few just; and when they prevail, the state may be truly called its own inferior and therefore bad; and when they are defeated, its own superior and therefore good."
refashioned: "Whether the better is ever truly conquered by the worse requires further discussion, so let's set it aside for now," the Stranger said. "But I understand your meaning clearly: citizens of the same race, living in the same city, may unjustly conspire together, and through sheer numbers they may overpower and enslave the just few. When the unjust majority prevails, the state is rightly called inferior to itself -- and therefore bad. When they are defeated, the state is superior to itself -- and therefore good."
elegant: The Stranger summarizes: when an unjust majority overpowers the just minority, the state is inferior to itself. When the just prevail, the state is superior to itself. Whether the better can truly be conquered by the worse remains an open question.
summary: The Stranger tables the deeper question of whether the better can truly be conquered by the worse, but confirms his understanding of Cleinias's view: a state ruled by an unjust majority is inferior to itself, while one where the just prevail is superior to itself.
distilled: A state where the unjust majority overpowers the just minority is 'inferior to itself' -- a condition worse than external defeat.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The question set aside -- whether the better can truly be conquered by the worse -- is addressed later in the Laws and extensively in the Republic (the 'decline of regimes' in Books 8-9). -- The concept of majority tyranny anticipates concerns raised by Tocqueville and Mill. -- The notion of a state 'inferior to itself' parallels the Republic's account of how democracies degenerate into tyrannies when the undisciplined many overpower the wise few (Republic 8.555b-562a). -- Setting aside difficult questions for later is a common Platonic dialectical strategy.

narrative:
"Your remark, Stranger, is a paradox, and yet we cannot possibly deny it," said Cleinias.

"Here is another case for consideration," said the Athenian Stranger. "In a family there may be several brothers, who are the offspring of a single pair; very possibly the majority of them may be unjust, and the just may be in a minority."

"Very possibly."

"And you and I ought not to raise a question of words as to whether this family and household are rightly said to be superior when they conquer, and inferior when they are conquered; for we are not now considering what may or may not be the proper or customary way of speaking, but we are considering the natural principles of right and wrong in laws."

"What you say, Stranger, is most true," said Cleinias.

"Quite excellent, in my opinion, as far as we have gone," added Megillus.

"Again; might there not be a judge over these brethren, of whom we were speaking?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"Your observation is paradoxical, Stranger, and yet we can't deny it," Cleinias acknowledged.

"Here's another case to consider," said the Stranger. "In a family with several brothers born to the same parents, it's entirely possible that the majority are unjust and the just are in the minority."

"Entirely possible."

"Now, we shouldn't get bogged down in verbal disputes about whether such a family is 'superior' when it conquers or 'inferior' when it's conquered. We're not debating proper usage of words -- we're investigating the natural principles of right and wrong in law."

"What you say is absolutely true," Cleinias agreed.

"Quite excellent reasoning, as far as we've come," Megillus added.

"Now then -- might there not be a judge appointed over these brothers we've been discussing?"

"Certainly."
elegant: The Stranger applies the argument to a family: if most brothers are unjust and few are just, the question of 'superiority' isn't about words but about natural principles of right and wrong. He then introduces the figure of a judge who must decide between them.
summary: The Stranger scales the argument down to a family of brothers, where the unjust may outnumber the just. He insists they focus not on verbal conventions but on natural principles of right and wrong, then introduces the idea of a judge who must arbitrate between them.
distilled: The question of justice versus injustice within a family introduces the need for a judge -- and by extension, a legislator.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The move from state to family mirrors the Republic's analogy between the soul and the city (Republic 2.368d). -- The insistence on natural principles over verbal conventions echoes Plato's persistent concern with the difference between names and realities (cf. Cratylus, Sophist 218b-c). -- The introduction of the 'judge' figure prepares the ground for the argument about the ideal legislator in 1.9. -- Megillus's brief approval ('Quite excellent') shows his role as the quieter, more agreeable interlocutor.

narrative: "Now, which would be the better judge," asked the Athenian Stranger, "one who destroyed the bad and appointed the good to govern themselves; or one who, while allowing the good to govern, let the bad live, and made them voluntarily submit? Or third, I suppose, in the scale of excellence might be placed a judge, who, finding the family distracted, not only did not destroy any one, but reconciled them to one another for ever after, and gave them laws which they mutually observed, and was able to keep them friends."
refashioned: "Which would be the better judge?" the Stranger asked. "One who destroys the bad and appoints the good to govern themselves? Or one who lets the good govern while allowing the bad to live, compelling them to submit voluntarily? Or would the highest rank go to a third kind of judge -- one who, finding the family in conflict, destroys no one but instead reconciles them all to one another permanently, gives them laws they mutually observe, and keeps them as friends?"
elegant: The Stranger ranks three types of judges: one who destroys the bad, one who subjugates them, and -- best of all -- one who reconciles all parties through shared law and lasting friendship.
summary: The Stranger presents a hierarchy of judges: the lowest destroys the wicked; the middle one subjugates them under the rule of the good; the best reconciles all parties, giving them mutually observed laws that preserve friendship permanently.
distilled: The ideal judge does not destroy or subjugate the wicked but reconciles all parties through law and friendship.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage is foundational for Plato's philosophy of law. The best legislator aims not at punishment or domination but at reconciliation and civic friendship (philia). -- This directly challenges the war-centric view Cleinias presented in 1.4. -- The concept of the reconciling judge anticipates restorative justice theory. -- Cf. Republic 1.351d-352a, where Socrates argues that even a band of thieves needs internal justice to function. -- The three-tier ranking (destruction, subjugation, reconciliation) moves from the most violent to the most harmonious -- a progression that mirrors Plato's hierarchy of souls and regimes.

narrative:
"The last would be by far the best sort of judge and legislator," said Cleinias.

"And yet the aim of all the laws which he gave would be the reverse of war," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"Very true."

"And will he who constitutes the state and orders the life of man have in view external war, or that kind of intestine war called civil, which no one, if he could prevent, would like to have occurring in his own state; and when occurring, every one would wish to be quit of as soon as possible?"

"He would have the latter chiefly in view."

"And would he prefer that this civil war should be terminated by the destruction of one of the parties, and by the victory of the other, or that peace and friendship should be re-established, and that, being reconciled, they should give their attention to foreign enemies?"

"Every one would desire the latter in the case of his own state."

"And would not that also be the desire of the legislator?"

"Certainly."

"And would not every one always make laws for the sake of the best?"

"To be sure."
refashioned:
"The last would be far and away the best kind of judge and legislator," Cleinias agreed.

"And yet all the laws such a judge would give would aim at the opposite of war," the Stranger pointed out.

"Very true."

"So when someone establishes a state and orders human life, will he focus on external war, or on that internal war called civil strife -- which no one would want in their own state, and which everyone would wish to end as quickly as possible?"

"He would focus primarily on civil strife."

"And would he prefer that civil conflict end through the destruction of one side and victory of the other? Or that peace and friendship be restored, so that once reconciled, the citizens can turn their attention to foreign enemies?"

"Everyone would want the latter for their own state."

"And wouldn't the legislator want the same?"

"Certainly."

"And wouldn't everyone always make laws for the sake of the best outcome?"

"Of course."
elegant: Cleinias agrees the reconciling judge is best -- then the Stranger springs the trap: such a judge's laws aim at the opposite of war. The true legislator's primary concern is not external enemies but internal harmony. Civil war should end not in one side's destruction but in reconciliation, freeing the united state to face external threats. Laws should always aim at the best.
summary: The Stranger draws out the implications of Cleinias's agreement: if the best legislator reconciles rather than conquers, then laws aim at peace, not war. The true legislator's chief concern is ending civil strife through reconciliation, not through destruction of one faction.
distilled: If the best legislator aims at reconciliation rather than conquest, then the true purpose of law is peace, not war.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger in Socratic question-and-answer. -- This is the pivot of the argument: Cleinias has been led to contradict his own thesis (1.4) that all laws aim at war. -- The Stranger's method is classically Socratic elenchus -- drawing out contradictions from the interlocutor's own premises. -- The prioritization of internal harmony over external military strength anticipates the Republic's argument that justice within the soul is more important than external success. -- 'Laws for the sake of the best' introduces the teleological principle that will govern the rest of the Laws: legislation must aim at complete virtue, not just one part of it.

narrative:
"But war, whether external or civil, is not the best, and the need of either is to be deprecated; but peace with one another, and good will, are best," said the Athenian Stranger. "Nor is the victory of the state over itself to be regarded as a really good thing, but as a necessity; a man might as well say that the body was in the best state when sick and purged by medicine, forgetting that there is also a state of the body which needs no purge. And in like manner no one can be a true statesman, whether he aims at the happiness of the individual or state, who looks only, or first of all, to external warfare; nor will he ever be a sound legislator who orders peace for the sake of war, and not war for the sake of peace."

"I suppose that there is truth, Stranger, in that remark of yours," replied Cleinias, "and yet I am greatly mistaken if war is not the entire aim and object of our own institutions, and also of the Lacedaemonian."
refashioned:
"War -- whether external or civil -- is not the best condition, and the need for either should be lamented," said the Stranger. "Peace and mutual goodwill are best. Nor should a state's victory over itself be seen as truly good -- only as a necessity. That would be like saying a body is in its best state when sick and being purged by medicine, forgetting that there is also a state of the body that needs no purge at all.

"In the same way, no one can be a true statesman -- whether aiming at the happiness of the individual or the state -- who looks primarily to external warfare. Nor will anyone be a sound legislator who designs peace for the sake of war, rather than war for the sake of peace."

"There is truth in what you say, Stranger," Cleinias replied, "and yet I would be greatly mistaken if war is not the entire aim and object of our institutions -- and the Spartan ones as well."
elegant: The Stranger delivers a key thesis: war is never the best condition. Peace and goodwill are best. A state that needs to conquer itself is like a sick body needing medicine -- not a sign of health. The true statesman orders war for the sake of peace, never peace for the sake of war. Cleinias concedes the point's truth but admits that both Cretan and Spartan institutions seem designed entirely for war.
summary: The Stranger argues that peace and goodwill are the highest aims, not war. A true legislator designs war for the sake of peace, not the reverse. Cleinias concedes the logic but acknowledges that both Cretan and Spartan institutions appear to have war as their sole object.
distilled: The true legislator orders war for the sake of peace, never peace for the sake of war.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The medical analogy (a purged body is not the healthiest body) is characteristically Platonic; cf. Gorgias 478a-b, where punishment is compared to medicine. -- 'War for the sake of peace' vs. 'peace for the sake of war' is one of the most quoted maxims from the Laws. Aristotle quotes it approvingly in Politics 7.14 (1333a35). -- Cleinias's partial concession ('there is truth... and yet') shows the dramatic tension: he recognizes the argument's force but cannot easily abandon his culture's war-centered worldview. -- This passage marks the conclusion of the first major argument: the refutation of war as the ultimate aim of legislation.

narrative:
"I dare say; but there is no reason why we should rudely quarrel with one another about your legislators, instead of gently questioning them, seeing that both we and they are equally in earnest," said the Athenian Stranger. "Please follow me and the argument closely. And first I will put forward Tyrtaeus, an Athenian by birth, but also a Spartan citizen, who of all men was most eager about war. Well, he says, 'I sing not, I care not, about any man, even if he were the richest of men, and possessed every good' -- and then he gives a whole list of them -- 'if he be not at all times a brave warrior.' I imagine that you, too, must have heard his poems; our Lacedaemonian friend has probably heard more than enough of them."

"Very true," said Megillus.

"And they have found their way from Lacedaemon to Crete," added Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Perhaps so," the Stranger replied, "but there is no reason to quarrel rudely about your lawgivers. Let us question them gently, since we are all equally serious in this inquiry. Follow me and the argument closely.

"First, I'll call upon Tyrtaeus -- Athenian by birth but also a Spartan citizen, and the man most passionate about war of anyone who ever lived. He writes: 'I sing not, I care not about any man, even if he were the richest and possessed every good' -- and he lists them all -- 'if he is not at all times a brave warrior.' I imagine you've heard his poems; our Spartan friend has probably heard more than enough of them."

"Very true," Megillus confirmed.

"And they've made their way from Sparta to Crete as well," Cleinias added.
elegant: The Stranger invokes Tyrtaeus -- an Athenian who became Sparta's most famous war poet -- who declared that no virtue matters unless a man is a brave warrior. Both Megillus and Cleinias know these poems well, confirming Tyrtaeus's influence across both their cultures.
summary: The Stranger introduces Tyrtaeus, the Spartan war poet, who declared that no quality -- wealth, beauty, or any other good -- matters unless a man is a brave warrior. Both Megillus and Cleinias confirm their deep familiarity with his works.
distilled: Tyrtaeus, the celebrated war poet, represents the view that military courage is the supreme virtue -- a position the Stranger will challenge.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Megillus, Cleinias. -- Tyrtaeus (7th century BCE) was a poet whose elegies were used to inspire Spartan soldiers. The tradition that he was Athenian by birth is mentioned by several ancient sources. -- The quoted lines paraphrase Tyrtaeus Fragment 12 (West). -- The Stranger's diplomatic approach ('gently questioning' rather than 'rudely quarreling') models the ideal philosophical discourse. -- By using a poet revered in both Sparta and Crete, the Stranger grounds his argument in authorities his interlocutors already accept -- a rhetorical strategy also seen in the Republic's treatment of Homer.

narrative:
"Come now and let us all join in asking this question of Tyrtaeus," said the Athenian Stranger. "'O most divine poet,' we will say to him, 'the excellent praise which you have bestowed on those who excel in war sufficiently proves that you are wise and good, and I and Megillus and Cleinias of Cnosus do, as I believe, entirely agree with you. But we should like to be quite sure that we are speaking of the same men; tell us, then, do you agree with us in thinking that there are two kinds of war?'

"A far inferior man to Tyrtaeus would have no difficulty in replying quite truly, that war is of two kinds -- one which is universally called civil war, and is as we were just now saying, of all wars the worst; the other, as we should all admit, in which we fall out with other nations who are of a different race, is a far milder form of warfare."
refashioned:
"Let us all put a question to Tyrtaeus," the Stranger proposed. "We would say to him: 'Most divine poet, your excellent praise of those who excel in war proves you are wise and good. Megillus, Cleinias, and I all agree with you. But we want to make sure we're talking about the same men. Tell us: do you agree that there are two kinds of war?'

"Even a far lesser man than Tyrtaeus could answer truthfully that war is of two kinds. One is universally called civil war -- as we were just saying, the worst of all wars. The other, where we clash with foreign nations of different races, is a far milder form of warfare."
elegant: The Stranger poses a question to the absent Tyrtaeus: does he recognize two kinds of war? Civil war is the worst; foreign war is milder. Any reasonable person would agree to this distinction -- but it reframes the poet's praise of martial courage.
summary: The Stranger constructs an imaginary dialogue with Tyrtaeus, asking whether the poet recognizes two kinds of war: civil war, which is the worst, and foreign war, which is comparatively milder. This distinction will be used to challenge whose courage Tyrtaeus truly praises.
distilled: The distinction between civil war (worst) and foreign war (milder) reframes the meaning of martial courage.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The device of addressing an absent poet is used by Socrates in the Protagoras (339a ff.) when he interrogates Simonides's poem. -- The distinction between civil and foreign war (stasis vs. polemos) was fundamental in Greek political thought. Thucydides treats stasis as the most destructive force in the Peloponnesian War (3.82-84). -- By establishing that civil war is worse, the Stranger prepares to argue that the virtue needed to prevent or resolve civil conflict is higher than the courage needed for external battles.

narrative:
"Certainly, far milder," agreed Cleinias.

"Well, now, when you praise and blame war in this high-flown strain, whom are you praising or blaming, and to which kind of war are you referring?" asked the Stranger. "I suppose that you must mean foreign war, if I am to judge from expressions of yours in which you say that you abominate those 'who refuse to look upon fields of blood, and will not draw near and strike at their enemies.'

"And we shall naturally go on to say to him -- 'You, Tyrtaeus, as it seems, praise those who distinguish themselves in external and foreign war'; and he must admit this."

"Evidently," said Cleinias.

"They are good; but we say that there are still better men whose virtue is displayed in the greatest of all battles. And we too have a poet whom we summon as a witness, Theognis, citizen of Megara in Sicily."
refashioned:
"Certainly, far milder," Cleinias agreed.

"So when you praise and blame war in such elevated terms, whom exactly are you praising or blaming, and which kind of war do you mean?" the Stranger pressed. "You must mean foreign war, judging from your words about despising those 'who refuse to look upon fields of blood and will not draw near to strike their enemies.'

"We would naturally say to Tyrtaeus: 'It seems you praise those who distinguish themselves in external, foreign war.' He would have to admit this."

"Evidently," said Cleinias.

"And they are good men. But we maintain there are even better men -- those whose virtue is displayed in the greatest of all battles. We too have a poet to call as witness: Theognis, citizen of Megara in Sicily."
elegant: The Stranger pins down that Tyrtaeus's praise applies specifically to foreign war. But there are better men -- those who prove their virtue in the greatest battle of all. The Stranger summons a counter-witness: the poet Theognis.
summary: The Stranger establishes that Tyrtaeus praises courage specifically in foreign war, then claims there are still better men who excel in the greater battle. He introduces Theognis as a counter-authority to Tyrtaeus.
distilled: Tyrtaeus praises courage in foreign war, but there exists a greater battle requiring a higher virtue.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The quoted line paraphrases Tyrtaeus Fragment 12.11-12. -- Theognis of Megara (6th century BCE) was an elegiac poet. The identification as 'citizen of Megara in Sicily' (Megara Hyblaea) rather than Megara in mainland Greece was debated in antiquity. -- The rhetorical strategy of pitting one revered poet against another allows the Stranger to make his philosophical point through culturally authoritative voices. -- 'The greatest of all battles' refers to the internal struggle for virtue, especially the battle against pleasures -- a theme developed extensively in what follows.

narrative:
"'Cyrnus, he who is faithful in a civil broil is worth his weight in gold and silver,'" quoted the Athenian Stranger. "And such an one is far better, as we affirm, than the other in a more difficult kind of war, much in the same degree as justice and temperance and wisdom, when united with courage, are better than courage only; for a man cannot be faithful and good in civil strife without having all virtue. But in the war of which Tyrtaeus speaks, many a mercenary soldier will take his stand and be ready to die at his post, and yet they are generally and almost without exception insolent, unjust, violent men, and the most senseless of human beings.

"You will ask what the conclusion is, and what I am seeking to prove: I maintain that the divine legislator of Crete, like any other who is worthy of consideration, will always and above all things in making laws have regard to the greatest virtue; which, according to Theognis, is loyalty in the hour of danger, and may be truly called perfect justice. Whereas, that virtue which Tyrtaeus highly praises is well enough, and was praised by the poet at the right time, yet in place and dignity may be said to be only fourth rate."

"Stranger, we are degrading our inspired lawgiver to a rank which is far beneath him," protested Cleinias.

"Nay, I think that we degrade not him but ourselves, if we imagine that Lycurgus and Minos laid down laws both in Lacedaemon and Crete mainly with a view to war."

"What ought we to say then?"
refashioned:
"'Cyrnus, the man who is faithful in civil conflict is worth his weight in gold and silver,'" the Stranger quoted. "Such a man is far better than the warrior Tyrtaeus praises, in a more difficult kind of war -- in the same degree that justice, temperance, and wisdom, united with courage, are better than courage alone. A man cannot be faithful and good in civil strife without possessing all virtue. But in the war Tyrtaeus describes, many mercenary soldiers will stand and die at their posts -- yet they are generally insolent, unjust, violent men, and the most senseless of human beings.

"Here is my conclusion: the divine Cretan legislator, like any legislator worthy of consideration, would always and above all else have regarded the greatest virtue when making laws. That virtue, according to Theognis, is faithfulness in the hour of danger -- what we may truly call complete justice. The virtue Tyrtaeus praises is admirable enough and was praised at the right time, but in rank and dignity it is only fourth-rate."

"Stranger, we are degrading our inspired lawgiver to a rank far beneath him!" Cleinias objected.

"No -- we degrade not him but ourselves, if we imagine that Lycurgus and Minos designed laws for Sparta and Crete mainly with a view to war."

"Then what ought we to say?"
elegant: Theognis declares that faithfulness in civil conflict is worth gold and silver. The Stranger argues this virtue surpasses Tyrtaeus's martial courage because it requires complete virtue -- justice, temperance, and wisdom combined with courage -- while mere battlefield bravery can be found even in mercenaries who are violent and unjust. A worthy legislator aims at the highest virtue, not just the fourth-ranked one. When Cleinias objects that this degrades the Cretan lawgiver, the Stranger replies: it degrades us to assume that Minos and Lycurgus aimed mainly at war.
summary: The Stranger argues that faithfulness in civil strife requires complete virtue -- justice, temperance, wisdom, and courage combined -- while battlefield courage alone can be found in the most worthless mercenaries. The true legislator aims at the highest virtue. Courage alone ranks only fourth.
distilled: Complete virtue -- justice, temperance, wisdom, and courage combined -- outranks courage alone, which is merely fourth in the hierarchy.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The Theognis quotation is from Theognidea 77-78. 'Cyrnus' was Theognis's beloved pupil. -- The hierarchy of virtues (justice > temperance > wisdom > courage, or justice as the combination of all with courage ranked fourth) differs from other Platonic orderings. In the Republic, wisdom leads; here, justice (understood as complete virtue) is supreme. -- The mercenary argument is powerful: courage without justice is common among the worst of men. Cf. Republic 4.430b on true courage requiring right belief. -- Cleinias's protest ('we are degrading our lawgiver') shows his cultural loyalty, but the Stranger deftly turns the argument: attributing higher aims to Minos actually honors him more. -- Lycurgus was the legendary Spartan lawgiver, as Minos was the legendary Cretan one.

narrative:
"What truth and what justice require of us, if I am not mistaken, when speaking in behalf of divine excellence," replied the Athenian Stranger, "is this: that the legislator when making his laws had in view not a part only, and this the lowest part of virtue, but all virtue, and that he devised classes of laws answering to the kinds of virtue; not in the way in which modern inventors of laws make the classes, for they only investigate and offer laws whenever a want is felt, and one man has a class of laws about allotments and heiresses, another about assaults; others about ten thousand other such matters.

"But we maintain that the right way of examining into laws is to proceed as we have now done, and I admired the spirit of your exposition; for you were quite right in beginning with virtue, and saying that this was the aim of the giver of the law, but I thought that you went wrong when you added that all his legislation had a view only to a part, and the least part of virtue, and this called forth my subsequent remarks. Will you allow me then to explain how I should have liked to have heard you expound the matter?"

"By all means," said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"What truth and justice require," the Stranger replied, "when speaking on behalf of divine excellence, is this: the legislator made his laws with all virtue in view, not just one part of it -- and certainly not the lowest part. He devised categories of laws corresponding to the different kinds of virtue. This is not how modern lawmakers operate -- they investigate and propose laws only when some need arises: one person has laws about property allotments and inheritance, another about assaults, others about ten thousand miscellaneous matters.

"We maintain that the right way to examine laws is to proceed as we've been doing. I admired the spirit of your presentation -- you were quite right to begin with virtue and to say that virtue was the lawgiver's aim. But I thought you went wrong when you claimed all his legislation aimed at only one part of virtue, and the least part at that. That is what prompted my subsequent remarks. Will you allow me to explain how I would have liked to hear you present the matter?"

"By all means," said Cleinias.
elegant: The true legislator designs laws for all virtue, not just one part. Modern lawmakers address problems piecemeal -- inheritance here, assault there. The right approach starts from virtue as a whole and derives categories of law from categories of virtue. Cleinias was right to identify virtue as the aim of law, but wrong to reduce it to courage alone.
summary: The Stranger argues that a true legislator designs laws answering to all virtues, not just courage. He contrasts this holistic approach with modern piecemeal lawmaking that addresses problems as they arise. He praises Cleinias for identifying virtue as the aim of law, but corrects him for reducing it to a single part.
distilled: Good legislation addresses all virtue systematically, not just courage; piecemeal lawmaking for isolated problems is the modern error.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The criticism of 'modern inventors of laws' who legislate piecemeal is a direct attack on the ad hoc Athenian legislative process, where laws were proposed in response to specific disputes. -- The idea that laws should correspond to categories of virtue is developed fully in what follows (1.17). -- This passage anticipates modern jurisprudential debates about whether law should be organized around principles or around problems. -- The Stranger's diplomacy continues: he praises Cleinias's starting point (virtue) while correcting his conclusion (courage only), modeling constructive philosophical critique.

narrative:
"You ought to have said, Stranger -- 'The Cretan laws are with reason famous among the Hellenes; for they fulfil the object of laws, which is to make those who use them happy; and they confer every sort of good,'" the Athenian Stranger began. "'Now goods are of two kinds: there are human and there are divine goods, and the human hang upon the divine; and the state which attains the greater, at the same time acquires the less, or, not having the greater, has neither.

"'Of the lesser goods the first is health, the second beauty, the third strength, including swiftness in running and bodily agility generally, and the fourth is wealth, not the blind god Pluto, but one who is keen of sight, if only he has wisdom for his companion. For wisdom is chief and leader of the divine class of goods, and next follows temperance; and from the union of these two with courage springs justice, and fourth in the scale of virtue is courage.

"'All these naturally take precedence of the other goods, and this is the order in which the legislator must place them, and after them he will enjoin the rest of his ordinances on the citizens with a view to these, the human looking to the divine, and the divine looking to their leader mind.

"'Some of his ordinances will relate to contracts of marriage which they make one with another, and then to the procreation and education of children, both male and female; the duty of the lawgiver will be to take charge of his citizens, in youth and age, and at every time of life, and to give them punishments and rewards; and in reference to all their intercourse with one another, he ought to consider their pains and pleasures and desires, and the vehemence of all their passions; he should keep a watch over them, and blame and praise them rightly by the mouth of the laws themselves.

"'Also with regard to anger and terror, and the other perturbations of the soul, which arise out of misfortune, and the deliverances from them which prosperity brings, and the experiences which come to men in diseases, or in war, or poverty, or the opposite of these; in all these states he should determine and teach what is the good and evil of the condition of each.'"
refashioned:
"Here is what you should have said," the Stranger began, speaking as Cleinias. "'The Cretan laws are rightly famous among the Greeks, for they fulfill the purpose of laws: to make those who live under them happy by providing every kind of good.

"'Goods are of two kinds -- divine and human -- and human goods depend upon divine ones. The state that attains the greater goods automatically acquires the lesser. Without the greater, it has neither.

"'Among lesser, human goods: first is health, second is beauty, third is strength -- including speed and physical agility -- and fourth is wealth. But not blind wealth: wealth with keen sight, guided by wisdom as its companion.

"'Among the divine goods: wisdom is the chief and leader. Second comes temperance. From the union of wisdom and temperance with courage springs justice. Fourth is courage itself.

"'All the divine goods naturally take precedence over the human ones. This is the order the legislator must follow. After establishing these priorities, he will design all other ordinances with these goods in view -- human goods looking to the divine, and the divine goods looking to their leader, the mind.

"'Some ordinances will concern marriage contracts, then the procreation and education of children, both male and female. The lawgiver's duty is to watch over citizens at every stage of life -- youth and age alike -- dispensing punishments and rewards. In all their dealings with one another, he must consider their pains, pleasures, desires, and the intensity of their passions, and he must blame and praise them rightly through the laws themselves.

"'He must also address anger, fear, and the other disturbances of the soul that arise from misfortune; the relief that prosperity brings; and the experiences that come from disease, war, poverty, or their opposites. In all these conditions, he must determine and teach what is good and what is evil.'"
elegant: The Stranger presents the model speech Cleinias should have given. Goods are twofold: divine (wisdom, temperance, justice, courage) and human (health, beauty, strength, wealth). Human goods depend on divine ones -- without the higher goods, the lower are worthless. The legislator must order everything by this hierarchy, addressing the whole of human life: marriage, education, rewards and punishments, and the full range of human passions and conditions. Wealth is valuable only when guided by wisdom.
summary: The Stranger provides a comprehensive model of what Cleinias should have said: goods are divine and human, with human goods depending on divine ones. The hierarchy places wisdom first, then temperance, justice, and courage among divine goods, and health, beauty, strength, and wealth among human ones. The legislator must address all aspects of human life according to this order.
distilled: Human goods -- health, beauty, strength, wealth -- depend entirely on divine goods: wisdom, temperance, justice, and courage, in that order.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger (speaking in Cleinias's voice as a model speech). -- This is one of the most important passages in the Laws, establishing the hierarchy of goods that governs the entire work. -- The distinction between divine and human goods echoes Euthydemus 279a-281e, where Socrates argues that conventional goods are only good when guided by wisdom. -- 'Blind Pluto' is a pun: Plutos (wealth) was associated with Pluto (god of the underworld, who is blind). Wealth without wisdom is blind. -- The hierarchy of divine goods (wisdom > temperance > justice > courage) differs from the Republic's emphasis on justice as the master virtue; here wisdom leads. -- The comprehensive scope of legislation outlined (marriage, education, emotions, all conditions of life) anticipates the detailed legislative program of Laws Books 4-12.

narrative:
"In the next place," continued the Athenian Stranger, "the legislator has to be careful how the citizens make their money and in what way they spend it, and to have an eye to their mutual contracts and dissolutions of contracts, whether voluntary or involuntary: he should see how they order all this, and consider where justice as well as injustice is found or is wanting in their several dealings with one another; and honour those who obey the law, and impose fixed penalties on those who disobey, until the round of civil life is ended, and the time has come for the consideration of the proper funeral rites and honours of the dead.

"And the lawgiver reviewing his work, will appoint guardians to preside over these things -- some who walk by intelligence, others by true opinion only, and then mind will bind together all his ordinances and show them to be in harmony with temperance and justice, and not with wealth or ambition.

"This is the spirit, Stranger, in which I was and am desirous that you should pursue the subject. And I want to know the nature of all these things, and how they are arranged in the laws of Zeus, as they are termed, and in those of the Pythian Apollo, which Minos and Lycurgus gave; and how the order of them is discovered to his eyes, who has experience in laws gained either by study or habit, although they are far from being self-evident to the rest of mankind like ourselves."

"How shall we proceed, Stranger?" asked Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Next," the Stranger continued, "the legislator must oversee how citizens earn and spend their money, and monitor their contracts and their dissolution -- whether voluntary or not. He must examine where justice or injustice appears in their dealings, honoring those who obey the law and imposing fixed penalties on those who disobey, until the cycle of civic life is complete and it comes time to consider funeral rites and honors for the dead.

"Finally, the lawgiver must review his entire work and appoint guardians to oversee it all -- some who act from intelligence, others from true opinion. Then mind will bind all the ordinances together and reveal them to be in harmony with temperance and justice, not with wealth or ambition.

"This is the approach in which I want us to pursue the subject. I want to understand the nature of all these things -- how they are arranged in the laws of Zeus and Pythian Apollo that Minos and Lycurgus gave -- and how their order becomes visible to someone experienced in law through study or practice, though it remains far from obvious to the rest of us."

"How shall we proceed, Stranger?" asked Cleinias.
elegant: The legislator must oversee the full economic and contractual life of citizens, from earning and spending to contracts and funerals. Guardians -- some guided by intelligence, others by true opinion -- must ensure all ordinances serve temperance and justice, not wealth or ambition. The underlying order of divine law is visible only to those experienced in legislation.
summary: The Stranger completes his model speech: the legislator must regulate economic life, contracts, and funeral rites, then appoint guardians to ensure all laws serve justice and temperance rather than wealth and ambition. The order within divine law is visible only to the experienced.
distilled: The legislator must oversee the entire cycle of civic life and appoint guardians to ensure all laws serve justice, not wealth or ambition.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The distinction between guardians who 'walk by intelligence' and those who act by 'true opinion' echoes the Republic's distinction between knowledge and true belief (Republic 5.476d-477a) and anticipates the Allegory of the Cave. -- 'Mind will bind together all his ordinances' -- nous as the unifying principle of law is a central theme of the Laws. -- The scope of legislation described (economic regulation, contract law, funerary law) corresponds closely to the actual legislative program in Laws Books 5-12. -- The phrase 'laws of Zeus' and 'Pythian Apollo' refers back to the opening claim of divine authorship (1.1). -- Cleinias's question 'How shall we proceed?' marks the transition from the theoretical framework to the detailed examination of specific virtues.

narrative: "I think that we must begin again as before, and first consider the habit of courage," said the Athenian Stranger, "and then we will go on and discuss another and then another form of virtue, if you please. In this way we shall have a model of the whole; and with these and similar discourses we will beguile the way. And when we have gone through all the virtues, we will show, by the grace of God, that the institutions of which I was speaking look to virtue."
refashioned: "I think we should start again where we began," said the Stranger, "first examining courage, and then moving on to discuss one form of virtue after another. This way we'll build a model of the whole. We'll pass the journey pleasantly with this kind of discussion. And once we've worked through all the virtues, we will show, by the grace of God, that the institutions I described truly aim at virtue."
elegant: The Stranger proposes a methodical plan: examine each virtue in turn -- beginning again with courage -- to build a complete model. By the journey's end, they will demonstrate that Cretan and Spartan institutions genuinely aim at virtue as a whole.
summary: The Stranger outlines their method: they will examine each virtue in turn, starting with courage, to construct a complete model. By the end of the journey, they aim to show that the institutions in question truly serve all of virtue.
distilled: The methodical plan: examine each virtue in turn, beginning with courage, to prove that true legislation serves complete virtue.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Begin again as before' signals a fresh start after the preliminary argument (1.1-1.18) has established the framework. -- The method of proceeding virtue by virtue parallels the Republic's examination of the four cardinal virtues in Book 4. -- 'Beguile the way' echoes the walking metaphor from 1.2, keeping the physical journey and intellectual journey intertwined. -- 'By the grace of God' reflects the pious tone that distinguishes the Laws from Plato's earlier, more rationalistic dialogues. -- The promise to show that institutions 'look to virtue' is the thesis the rest of the Laws aims to demonstrate.

narrative:
"Very good; and suppose that you first criticize this praiser of Zeus and the laws of Crete," said Megillus.

"I will try to criticize you and myself, as well as him, for the argument is a common concern," replied the Athenian Stranger. "Tell me -- were not first the syssitia, and secondly the gymnasia, invented by your legislator with a view to war?"

"Yes."

"And what comes third, and what fourth? For that, I think, is the sort of enumeration which ought to be made of the remaining parts of virtue, no matter whether you call them parts or what their name is, provided the meaning is clear."

"Then I, or any other Lacedaemonian, would reply that hunting is third in order," said Megillus.

"Let us see if we can discover what comes fourth and fifth."
refashioned:
"Very well -- suppose you start by critiquing our friend the praiser of Zeus and the Cretan laws," Megillus suggested.

"I'll try to criticize all of us -- you, myself, and him -- since the argument is our shared concern," the Stranger replied. "Tell me: weren't the common meals first, and the gymnasia second, among your legislator's institutions designed for war?"

"Yes."

"And what comes third and fourth? That's the kind of enumeration we should make for the remaining parts of virtue -- whether you call them 'parts' or something else, as long as the meaning is clear."

"I, or any other Spartan, would say hunting comes third," said Megillus.

"Let's see if we can figure out what comes fourth and fifth."
elegant: The Stranger proposes to criticize all three of them equally, then enumerates Cretan and Spartan institutions designed for war: common meals first, gymnasia second, hunting third. He asks what comes fourth and fifth in the training of courage.
summary: Megillus invites the Stranger to critique Cretan laws. The Stranger agrees to criticize all three of them, then enumerates war-related institutions: common meals, gymnasia, and hunting. He asks what further institutions exist.
distilled: The enumeration of military institutions -- meals, gymnastics, hunting -- begins the specific examination of how Cretan and Spartan laws cultivate courage.
notes: Speakers: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- Syssitia (common meals) and gymnasia were the foundational institutions of both Spartan and Cretan civic life. -- Hunting was a central part of Spartan education (the agoge). Xenophon's Cynegeticus praises hunting as military training. -- The insistence that naming conventions don't matter ('whether you call them parts or what') reflects Plato's characteristic concern with substance over terminology (cf. 1.8, Sophist 218c). -- This passage begins the detailed examination of courage-training that leads to the discussion of pleasure and drinking.

narrative:
"I think that I can get as far as the fourth head, which is the frequent endurance of pain, exhibited among us Spartans in certain hand-to-hand fights," said Megillus, "also in stealing with the prospect of getting a good beating; there is, too, the so-called Crypteia, or secret service, in which wonderful endurance is shown -- our people wander over the whole country by day and by night, and even in winter have not a shoe to their foot, and are without beds to lie upon, and have to attend upon themselves.

"Marvellous, too, is the endurance which our citizens show in their naked exercises, contending against the violent summer heat; and there are many similar practices, to speak of which in detail would be endless."
refashioned:
"I can get to the fourth category," said Megillus. "It is the frequent endurance of pain. Among us Spartans this appears in certain hand-to-hand combats, and in ritual theft where you're beaten if caught. Then there's the Crypteia -- the secret service -- which demands extraordinary endurance: our young men roam the entire countryside day and night, even in winter, barefoot and without beds, entirely self-reliant.

"There's also the remarkable endurance shown in our naked exercises, contending against brutal summer heat. And there are countless similar practices -- to describe them all would take forever."
elegant: Megillus identifies the fourth element of Spartan training: the systematic endurance of pain -- through ritual combat, ritual theft with beatings, the Crypteia (barefoot wilderness survival in all seasons), and naked exercises in extreme heat.
summary: Megillus describes the fourth component of Spartan training: endurance of pain. This includes ritual fighting, theft with the risk of beating, the Crypteia (secret service requiring barefoot wandering in all weather), and naked exercises in extreme heat.
distilled: Spartan courage-training centers on systematic endurance of pain through ritual combat, the Crypteia, and exposure to physical extremes.
notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- The Crypteia was a Spartan institution where young men were sent into the countryside with minimal provisions, reportedly to prove their endurance and to terrorize the helot population. Plutarch (Life of Lycurgus 28) describes it in detail. -- The ritual stealing was part of the Spartan agoge (education system): boys were encouraged to steal food but beaten if caught, training both cunning and pain tolerance. Xenophon describes this in Constitution of the Lacedaemonians 2.6-8. -- The naked exercises (gymnos = naked, hence 'gymnasium') in extreme heat reflect the Spartan principle that comfort breeds weakness. -- The Stranger will use this comprehensive picture of pain-training to ask the critical question: where is the corresponding training against pleasure?

narrative:
"Excellent, O Lacedaemonian Stranger," said the Athenian. "But how ought we to define courage? Is it to be regarded only as a combat against fears and pains, or also against desires and pleasures, and against flatteries; which exercise such a tremendous power, that they make the hearts even of respectable citizens to melt like wax?"

"I should say the latter," replied Megillus.

"In what preceded, as you will remember, our Cnosian friend was speaking of a man or a city being inferior to themselves. Were you not, Cleinias?"

"I was."

"Now, which is in the truest sense inferior, the man who is overcome by pleasure or by pain?"

"I should say the man who is overcome by pleasure," replied Cleinias, "for all men deem him to be inferior in a more disgraceful sense, than the other who is overcome by pain."

"But surely the lawgivers of Crete and Lacedaemon have not legislated for a courage which is lame of one leg, able only to meet attacks which come from the left, but impotent against the insidious flatteries which come from the right?"

"Able to meet both, I should say," replied Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Excellent," said the Stranger. "But how should we define courage? Is it only a fight against fears and pains? Or does it also include the battle against desires, pleasures, and flatteries -- which exert such tremendous power that they make even respectable citizens' hearts melt like wax?"

"The latter," Megillus answered.

"You'll recall that our Cretan friend was speaking earlier about a man or a city being inferior to itself. Weren't you, Cleinias?"

"I was."

"Now tell me: who is truly more inferior -- the man overcome by pleasure or the man overcome by pain?"

"The man overcome by pleasure," Cleinias replied. "Everyone considers his condition more disgraceful than that of the man overcome by pain."

"Then surely the Cretan and Spartan lawgivers didn't design a courage that is lame in one leg -- able to resist attacks from the left but powerless against the insidious flatteries coming from the right?"

"Able to meet both, I should say."
elegant: The Stranger redefines courage: it is not merely resistance to pain and fear, but also resistance to pleasure, desire, and flattery -- forces that melt even respectable hearts like wax. Being overcome by pleasure is more disgraceful than being overcome by pain. True courage must not be 'lame of one leg,' able to fight pain but helpless against pleasure.
summary: The Stranger expands the definition of courage beyond resistance to pain to include resistance to pleasure and desire. Cleinias agrees that being overcome by pleasure is more disgraceful than being overcome by pain, and that true legislation must train against both.
distilled: Courage is not just resistance to pain but equally resistance to pleasure -- and yielding to pleasure is the more disgraceful defeat.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Megillus, Cleinias. -- The image of courage 'lame of one leg' is vivid and memorable -- a characteristically Platonic metaphor. -- The idea that pleasure is more dangerous than pain because it corrupts more insidiously parallels the Republic's treatment of the 'democratic' soul seduced by desires (Republic 8.559d-561a). -- 'Hearts melt like wax' echoes Homer (Iliad 22.501; Odyssey 19.205-208). -- This passage is the pivot toward the drinking discussion: if courage must be trained against pleasure, what institution trains this? The answer will be the properly conducted symposium. -- Cf. Protagoras 353c-354e, where Socrates argues that being overcome by pleasure is really a failure of knowledge.

narrative: "Then let me once more ask," said the Athenian Stranger, "what institutions have you in either of your states which give a taste of pleasures, and do not avoid them any more than they avoid pains; but which set a person in the midst of them, and compel or induce him by the prospect of reward to get the better of them? Where is an ordinance about pleasure similar to that about pain to be found in your laws? Tell me what there is of this nature among you -- what is there which makes your citizen equally brave against pleasure and pain, conquering what they ought to conquer, and superior to the enemies who are most dangerous and nearest home?"
refashioned: "Let me ask again," said the Stranger. "What institutions exist in either of your states that expose citizens to pleasures -- not avoiding them any more than they avoid pains -- placing a person in the middle of temptation and compelling or motivating him to overcome them? Where in your laws is there an ordinance about pleasure corresponding to the ones about pain? Tell me: what makes your citizens equally brave against pleasure and pain, conquering what they should conquer, and proving superior to the enemies that are most dangerous and closest to home?"
elegant: The Stranger poses the decisive challenge: your laws train people to endure pain, but where are the institutions that train them to resist pleasure? What makes a citizen equally courageous against the more dangerous, closer enemies of desire and temptation?
summary: The Stranger asks the crucial question: where are the institutions that train citizens to resist pleasure as they are trained to resist pain? What in Cretan or Spartan law creates equal courage against desire and temptation?
distilled: The critical gap in Cretan and Spartan law: extensive training against pain, but nothing comparable against the more dangerous enemy of pleasure.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Enemies who are most dangerous and nearest home' -- pleasure is the internal enemy, more threatening than external foes. This echoes the earlier argument (1.6) that the greatest war is the war within oneself. -- The phrase 'give a taste of pleasures' without avoiding them anticipates the argument for controlled exposure to wine as a training ground for temperance. -- This question has no good answer within the Spartan system, which relied on avoidance and prohibition of pleasure rather than controlled engagement with it. -- The Stranger's approach (training through exposure rather than avoidance) has parallels in cognitive-behavioral therapy and modern resilience training.

narrative:
"I was able to tell you, Stranger, many laws which were directed against pain; but I do not know that I can point out any great or obvious examples of similar institutions which are concerned with pleasure; there are some lesser provisions, however, which I might mention," said Megillus.

"Neither can I show anything of that sort which is at all equally prominent in the Cretan laws," added Cleinias.

"No wonder, my dear friends," said the Athenian Stranger, "and if, as is very likely, in our search after the true and good, one of us may have to censure the laws of the others, we must not be offended, but take kindly what another says."

"You are quite right, Athenian Stranger, and we will do as you say," replied Cleinias.

"At our time of life, Cleinias, there should be no feeling of irritation."

"Certainly not."
refashioned:
"I was able to list many laws directed against pain," Megillus admitted, "but I can't point to any great or obvious institutions concerned with pleasure. There are some minor provisions I could mention."

"Nor can I show anything equally prominent in Cretan law," Cleinias added.

"No wonder, my friends," said the Stranger. "And if, as is likely in our search for truth and goodness, one of us has to criticize another's laws, we must not take offense but receive it graciously."

"You're quite right, Stranger, and we'll do as you say," Cleinias replied.

"At our age, Cleinias, there should be no room for irritation."

"Certainly not."
elegant: Both Megillus and Cleinias concede they cannot identify major institutions for training against pleasure. The Stranger gently prepares them for criticism, asking that they receive it without offense -- befitting men of their age.
summary: Both Megillus and Cleinias admit they cannot point to significant institutions that train citizens against pleasure, only against pain. The Stranger diplomatically asks that they accept mutual criticism without offense as they pursue the truth together.
distilled: Neither Sparta nor Crete has significant institutions for training against pleasure -- a critical gap both interlocutors acknowledge.
notes: Speakers: Megillus, Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. -- Megillus's admission is significant: the most militaristic Greek state has no systematic pleasure-training. -- The Stranger's request for open-minded reception of criticism models the ideal conditions for philosophical inquiry. -- 'At our time of life' -- the speakers' old age licenses frank discussion (cf. 1.25-1.26 on the law permitting only elders to critique legislation). -- The gap identified here -- training against pain but not against pleasure -- is the argumentative opening through which the Stranger will introduce the value of regulated drinking.

narrative: "I will not at present determine whether he who censures the Cretan or Lacedaemonian polities is right or wrong," said the Athenian Stranger. "But I believe that I can tell better than either of you what the many say about them. For assuming that you have reasonably good laws, one of the best of them will be the law forbidding any young men to enquire which of them are right or wrong; but with one mouth and one voice they must all agree that the laws are all good, for they came from God; and any one who says the contrary is not to be listened to. But an old man who remarks any defect in your laws may communicate his observation to a ruler or to an equal in years when no young man is present."
refashioned: "I won't determine right now whether anyone who criticizes Cretan or Spartan institutions is correct or not," said the Stranger. "But I think I understand better than either of you what most people say about them. Assuming your laws are reasonably good, one of the best among them must be the law forbidding young men from questioning whether any law is right or wrong. With one voice they must all declare that the laws are good, because they came from God, and anyone who says otherwise must not be heard. But an old man who notices a defect in your laws may share his observation with a ruler or an age-mate, provided no young person is present."
elegant: The Stranger identifies a wise provision: young men are forbidden from questioning laws (which must be accepted as divinely given), but old men may criticize them privately to rulers or peers when no youth is present.
