summary: The Stranger describes a notable provision in Cretan and Spartan law: young men must accept all laws as divinely good without question, but old men may privately share criticisms with rulers or peers when no young person is present.
distilled: The young must accept laws without question, but the old may privately critique them -- protecting both civic order and the possibility of reform.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This passage reflects a sophisticated understanding of social stability: public questioning of laws by the young breeds instability, but the inability to critique laws at all prevents reform. The solution is age-restricted private criticism. -- The 'came from God' justification echoes the opening (1.1) and raises the question of whether divine authorship precludes improvement. -- Cf. Republic 3.389b-c on the permissibility of noble lies for social cohesion. -- The provision anticipates the Laws's own structure: three old men walking privately, with no young audience, conducting exactly the kind of elder critique described here.

narrative:
"Exactly so, Stranger; and like a diviner, although not there at the time, you seem to me quite to have hit the meaning of the legislator, and to say what is most true," said Cleinias.

"As there are no young men present, and the legislator has given old men free licence, there will be no impropriety in our discussing these very matters now that we are alone," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"True," said Cleinias. "And therefore you may be as free as you like in your censure of our laws, for there is no discredit in knowing what is wrong; he who receives what is said in a generous and friendly spirit will be all the better for it."
refashioned:
"Exactly right, Stranger. Like a prophet, even though you weren't there, you seem to have grasped the legislator's intention perfectly and spoken the truth," said Cleinias.

"Since no young men are present, and the legislator has given old men this freedom, there will be nothing improper in our discussing these matters privately," the Stranger replied.

"True," Cleinias agreed. "So feel free to criticize our laws as openly as you wish. There is no shame in recognizing what is wrong. Anyone who receives such observations in a generous and friendly spirit will only benefit from them."
elegant: Cleinias praises the Stranger's insight into Cretan law. Since they are three old men walking alone -- exactly the circumstance the law permits for critique -- they may freely examine their laws. There is no shame in learning what is wrong.
summary: Cleinias praises the Stranger's insight and confirms they are in the exact circumstance the law permits for critique: old men alone, without young audiences. He invites full freedom of criticism, noting there is no shame in recognizing errors.
distilled: Three old men alone on a road -- the perfect circumstance for the kind of frank legal critique that their own laws permit.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's comparison of the Stranger to a 'diviner' (mantis) is significant: it attributes a quasi-prophetic quality to philosophical insight. -- The dramatic situation perfectly fulfills the conditions just described in 1.25: three elderly men in private conversation with no young men present. Plato has constructed the dramatic setting to license exactly the critique he wants to conduct. -- 'There is no discredit in knowing what is wrong' expresses a philosophical openness that contrasts with the typical Greek competitive defensiveness about one's city's institutions. -- This exchange establishes the ground rules for the rest of the dialogue: frank, friendly, truth-seeking inquiry.

narrative:
"Very good; however, I am not going to say anything against your laws until to the best of my ability I have examined them, but I am going to raise doubts about them," said the Athenian Stranger. "For you are the only people known to us, whether Greek or barbarian, whom the legislator commanded to eschew all great pleasures and amusements and never to touch them; whereas in the matter of pains or fears which we have just been discussing, he thought that they who from infancy had always avoided pains and fears and sorrows, when they were compelled to face them would run away from those who were hardened in them, and would become their subjects.

"Now the legislator ought to have considered that this was equally true of pleasure; he should have said to himself, that if our citizens are from their youth upward unacquainted with the greatest pleasures, and unused to endure amid the temptations of pleasure, and are not disciplined to refrain from all things evil, the sweet feeling of pleasure will overcome them just as fear would overcome the former class; and in another, and even a worse manner, they will be the slaves of those who are able to endure amid pleasures, and have had the opportunity of enjoying them, they being often the worst of mankind.

"One half of their souls will be a slave, the other half free; and they will not be worthy to be called in the true sense men and freemen. Tell me whether you assent to my words?"
refashioned:
"Very well -- but I won't speak against your laws until I've examined them as thoroughly as I can. I will, however, raise doubts," said the Stranger.

"You are the only people known to us, Greek or barbarian, whose legislator commanded the complete avoidance of great pleasures and amusements. Yet in the matter of pains and fears we just discussed, your legislator understood that those who have always avoided pain from infancy, when forced to face it, will flee from those hardened to it and become their subjects.

"The legislator should have recognized that the same principle applies to pleasure. He should have told himself: if our citizens grow up unacquainted with the greatest pleasures, unaccustomed to enduring temptation, and undisciplined in refusing evil, then the sweet pull of pleasure will overcome them -- just as fear overcomes the untrained. And in an even worse way, they will become slaves to those who can endure amid pleasures and have had the chance to enjoy them, even though such people are often the worst of mankind.

"Half their souls will be enslaved, the other half free -- and they will not deserve to be called truly free men. Do you agree?"
elegant: The Stranger identifies a fatal inconsistency: the Cretan and Spartan lawgivers understood that avoiding pain creates vulnerability to pain, yet they commanded total avoidance of pleasure. By the same logic, citizens raised without exposure to temptation become slaves to pleasure when they encounter it -- and slaves specifically to those experienced in pleasure, who are often the worst people. Half their soul is free, half enslaved: they cannot be called truly free.
summary: The Stranger argues that Cretan and Spartan laws contain a critical inconsistency: they train against pain through exposure but command total avoidance of pleasure. Citizens raised without experiencing temptation become enslaved by pleasure when they encounter it, leaving half their souls unfree.
distilled: Avoiding pleasure entirely creates the same vulnerability as avoiding pain -- citizens untrained against temptation become enslaved by it.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Half slave, half free' echoes the Republic's description of the internally divided soul (Republic 4.440a-441c). -- The argument that avoidance creates vulnerability rather than strength is a sophisticated psychological insight. It anticipates modern exposure therapy and the concept of 'antifragility.' -- 'Whether Greek or barbarian' universalizes the argument beyond Greek parochialism. -- The Stranger distinguishes between 'saying something against' laws and 'raising doubts' -- a careful methodological distinction between criticism and inquiry. -- The claim that those experienced in pleasure are 'often the worst of mankind' refers to the corrupting effect of unregulated luxury (cf. Republic 8-9 on tyrannical personalities).

narrative:
"On first hearing, what you say appears to be the truth; but to be hasty in coming to a conclusion about such important matters would be very childish and simple," said Cleinias.

"Suppose, Cleinias and Megillus, that we consider the virtue which follows next of those which we intended to discuss -- for after courage comes temperance," said the Athenian Stranger. "What institutions shall we find relating to temperance, either in Crete or Lacedaemon, which, like your military institutions, differ from those of any ordinary state?"

"That is not an easy question to answer," said Megillus, "still I should say that the common meals and gymnastic exercises have been excellently devised for the promotion both of temperance and courage."
refashioned:
"At first hearing, what you say seems true," Cleinias replied, "but it would be childish and naive to rush to conclusions on such important matters."

"Then let us consider the next virtue in our sequence," said the Stranger. "After courage comes temperance. What institutions in Crete or Sparta relate to temperance, just as your military institutions relate to courage -- something that distinguishes you from ordinary states?"

"That's not an easy question to answer," Megillus said, "but I would say that the common meals and gymnastic exercises were excellently designed to promote both temperance and courage."
elegant: Cleinias accepts the argument provisionally but urges caution. The Stranger turns to the next virtue: temperance. Megillus can only offer the same institutions already discussed -- common meals and gymnastics -- as serving both courage and temperance.
summary: Cleinias cautiously accepts the argument about pleasure. The Stranger shifts to temperance, asking what distinctive institutions promote it. Megillus can only cite the common meals and gymnastics already discussed for courage.
distilled: When asked about institutions for temperance, Megillus can only name the same ones designed for courage -- revealing the gap in Spartan legislation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger, Megillus. -- Cleinias's caution ('childish and simple to be hasty') shows him as a more careful thinker than typical Socratic interlocutors who agree too quickly. -- The transition from courage to temperance follows the order established in 1.17 (wisdom, temperance, justice, courage -- working backward from courage). -- Megillus's inability to name distinct temperance-institutions confirms the Stranger's critique: Spartan law is comprehensive on courage but thin on other virtues. -- The common meals (syssitia) did regulate food consumption and thus had a temperance dimension, but Megillus cannot articulate this distinctly.

narrative:
"There seems to be a difficulty, Stranger, with regard to states, in making words and facts coincide so that there can be no dispute about them," said the Athenian Stranger. "As in the human body, the regimen which does good in one way does harm in another; and we can hardly say that any one course of treatment is adapted to a particular constitution.

"Now the gymnasia and common meals do a great deal of good, and yet they are a source of evil in civil troubles; as is shown in the case of the Milesian, and Boeotian, and Thurian youth, among whom these institutions seem always to have had a tendency to degrade the ancient and natural custom of love below the level, not only of man, but of the beasts. The charge may be fairly brought against your cities above all others, and is true also of most other states which especially cultivate gymnastics.

"Whether such matters are to be regarded jestingly or seriously, I think that the pleasure is to be deemed natural which arises out of the intercourse between men and women; but that the intercourse of men with men, or of women with women, is contrary to nature, and that the bold attempt was originally due to unbridled lust.

"The Cretans are always accused of having invented the story of Ganymede and Zeus because they wanted to justify themselves in the enjoyment of unnatural pleasures by the practice of the god whom they believe to have been their lawgiver.

"Leaving the story, we may observe that any speculation about laws turns almost entirely on pleasure and pain, both in states and in individuals: these are two fountains which nature lets flow, and he who draws from them where and when, and as much as he ought, is happy; and this holds of men and animals -- of individuals as well as states; and he who indulges in them ignorantly and at the wrong time, is the reverse of happy."
refashioned:
"There is a difficulty with states," the Stranger observed, "in making words and facts align without dispute. Like the human body, where a treatment that helps in one way can harm in another, we can hardly say that any single course of action is perfectly suited to every constitution.

"Gymnasia and common meals produce great benefits, yet they also breed evil during civil unrest -- as seen among the youth of Miletus, Boeotia, and Thurii, where these institutions have tended to degrade natural love below the level not just of humans, but of animals. This charge applies especially to your cities, and also to most other states that emphasize gymnastics.

"Whether we treat this lightly or seriously, I hold that the pleasure arising from the union of men and women is natural, while sexual relations between men and men, or women and women, are contrary to nature -- the result of unbridled desire.

"The Cretans are always accused of inventing the story of Ganymede and Zeus to justify their practices by attributing them to their divine lawgiver.

"Setting that story aside, we should note that all reflection about laws comes down to pleasure and pain in both states and individuals. These are two fountains that nature lets flow, and whoever draws from them at the right time, in the right place, and in the right measure, is happy. This holds for people and animals, individuals and states alike. Whoever indulges ignorantly or at the wrong time lives the opposite of a happy life."
elegant: Institutions have unintended consequences: gymnasia and common meals promote virtue but also create conditions for vice, particularly unnatural sexual practices. The Stranger condemns same-sex relations as contrary to nature. Setting aside this controversy, the deeper point emerges: all legislation ultimately concerns pleasure and pain. These are nature's two fountains -- and happiness depends on drawing from them in the right measure, at the right time.
summary: The Stranger acknowledges that institutions like gymnasia and common meals have both good and bad effects, citing their association with unnatural sexual practices. He then articulates the fundamental insight: all law ultimately concerns the regulation of pleasure and pain, nature's two fountains, and happiness depends on drawing from them correctly.
distilled: All legislation ultimately comes down to the regulation of pleasure and pain -- nature's two fountains from which we must draw in the right measure.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The discussion of homosexuality is among the most controversial passages in Plato. The Stranger calls it 'contrary to nature' (para phusin), contrasting with the more nuanced treatment in the Symposium and Phaedrus. The Laws represents Plato's strictest position on this topic. -- The Ganymede myth (Zeus abducting a beautiful boy) was widely used in Greek culture to justify pederasty. The Stranger's claim that the Cretans invented it is polemical. -- Miletus, Boeotia, and Thurii were proverbially associated with sexual license. -- The 'two fountains' metaphor is powerful and original: pleasure and pain as natural resources that must be regulated. -- The idea that happiness depends on drawing pleasure and pain 'where and when, and as much as one ought' is the Aristotelian doctrine of the mean avant la lettre (cf. Nicomachean Ethics 2.6).

narrative:
"I admit, Stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know what to say in answer to you," said Megillus, "but still I think that the Spartan lawgiver was quite right in forbidding pleasure. Of the Cretan laws, I shall leave the defence to my Cnosian friend.

"But the laws of Sparta, in as far as they relate to pleasure, appear to me to be the best in the world; for that which leads mankind in general into the wildest pleasure and licence, and every other folly, the law has clean driven out; and neither in the country nor in towns which are under the control of Sparta, will you find revelries and the many incitements of every kind of pleasure which accompany them; and any one who meets a drunken and disorderly person, will immediately have him most severely punished, and will not let him off on any pretence, not even at the time of a Dionysiac festival; although I have remarked that this may happen at your performances 'on the cart,' as they are called; and among our Tarentine colonists I have seen the whole city drunk at a Dionysiac festival; but nothing of the sort happens among us."
refashioned:
"I admit, Stranger, that your words are well spoken, and I hardly know how to answer," said Megillus. "But I still think the Spartan lawgiver was absolutely right to forbid pleasure. I'll leave the defense of Cretan laws to my friend from Knossos.

"Spartan laws concerning pleasure seem to me the best in the world. Everything that leads people into wild pleasure, license, and folly has been completely driven out. In no town or countryside under Spartan control will you find revelries or the countless incitements to pleasure that accompany them. Anyone who encounters a drunken, disorderly person will have them severely punished immediately, with no excuses accepted -- not even during a Dionysiac festival.

"I've noticed this does happen at your Athenian performances 'on the cart,' as they're called. And among our Tarentine colonists, I've seen an entire city drunk at a Dionysiac festival. But nothing of the sort happens among us."
elegant: Megillus defends Sparta's total prohibition of pleasure: no revelries, no drunkenness, no exceptions even during Dionysiac festivals. He contrasts Spartan discipline with Athenian festival excesses and the spectacle of their Tarentine colonists getting drunk city-wide. The Spartan approach is simple: drive pleasure out completely.
summary: Megillus defends Sparta's strict prohibition of pleasure, citing the complete absence of revelry, drunkenness, and disorder in Spartan territory. He contrasts this with Athenian festival excesses and the public drunkenness he witnessed among Sparta's Tarentine colonists.
distilled: Megillus defends Sparta's solution to pleasure: total prohibition, with severe punishment for any drunkenness or revelry, no exceptions.
notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- 'Performances on the cart' refers to the Athenian Anthesteria festival, where revelers rode on carts and engaged in ribald humor. -- Tarentum (modern Taranto in Italy) was a Spartan colony founded c. 706 BCE. That even a Spartan colony abandoned Spartan discipline regarding drink is telling. -- Megillus's defense represents the prohibitionist position: the best way to handle pleasure is to eliminate it entirely. The Stranger will argue against this in what follows. -- The Dionysiac festival connection is thematically important: Dionysus is the god of wine, and the Laws will develop an argument for the educational value of properly regulated drinking -- essentially a philosophical rehabilitation of Dionysian practice.

narrative:
"O Lacedaemonian Stranger, these festivities are praiseworthy where there is a spirit of endurance, but are very senseless when they are under no regulations," said the Athenian Stranger. "In order to retaliate, an Athenian has only to point out the licence which exists among your women. To all such accusations, whether they are brought against the Tarentines, or us, or you, there is one answer which exonerates the practice in question from impropriety. When a stranger expresses wonder at the singularity of what he sees, any inhabitant will naturally answer him: 'Wonder not, O stranger; this is our custom, and you may very likely have some other custom about the same things.'

"Now we are speaking, my friends, not about men in general, but about the merits and defects of the lawgivers themselves. Let us then discourse a little more at length about intoxication, which is a very important subject, and will seriously task the discrimination of the legislator. I am not speaking of drinking, or not drinking, wine at all, but of intoxication. Are we to follow the custom of the Scythians, and Persians, and Carthaginians, and Celts, and Iberians, and Thracians, who are all warlike nations, or that of your countrymen, for they, as you say, altogether abstain? But the Scythians and Thracians, both men and women, drink unmixed wine, which they pour on their garments, and this they think a happy and glorious institution. The Persians, again, are much given to other practices of luxury which you reject, but they have more moderation in them than the Thracians and Scythians."

"O best of men, we have only to take arms into our hands, and we send all these nations flying before us," replied Megillus.
refashioned:
"These festivities are praiseworthy where there is endurance involved, but senseless when unregulated," the Stranger replied. "And to retaliate, an Athenian need only point out the license existing among your women. But to all such accusations -- against Tarentines, Athenians, or Spartans -- there is one answer: 'Don't be surprised, stranger; this is our custom, and you likely have different ones.'

"But we are not speaking about customs in general. We are evaluating the merits and defects of lawgivers themselves. So let us discuss intoxication at greater length -- it is a genuinely important subject that will seriously test a legislator's judgment.

"I am not asking whether to drink wine or not. I am asking about intoxication. Should we follow the Scythians, Persians, Carthaginians, Celts, Iberians, and Thracians -- all warlike peoples? Or your Spartan custom of total abstinence? The Scythians and Thracians, men and women alike, drink unmixed wine and pour it on their clothing, considering this a happy and glorious practice. The Persians indulge heavily in other luxuries you reject, but drink with more moderation than the Scythians and Thracians."

"Best of men," Megillus retorted, "we simply take up arms and put all those nations to flight."
elegant: The Stranger reframes the debate: the question is not whether to drink, but how to handle intoxication. Different warlike nations take different approaches -- Scythians drink wildly, Persians indulge moderately, Spartans abstain entirely. Cultural customs prove nothing by themselves; what matters is the legislator's judgment. When Megillus boasts of Spartan military superiority, the Stranger will show this is irrelevant to the argument.
summary: The Stranger distinguishes between drinking and intoxication, surveying different cultures' approaches: Scythian excess, Persian moderation, Spartan abstinence. He insists the question is about legislative judgment, not cultural custom. Megillus responds with a boast about Spartan military supremacy.
distilled: The question is not whether to drink but how a legislator should handle intoxication -- and military success does not settle the matter.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- 'Licence among your women' refers to the relative freedom of Spartan women, which scandalized other Greeks. Aristotle criticizes it in Politics 2.9 (1269b-1270a). -- The ethnographic survey (Scythians, Persians, Carthaginians, Celts, Iberians, Thracians) shows the Stranger's cosmopolitan knowledge and suggests that the question of drink is universal. -- 'Unmixed wine' (akratos) was considered barbaric by Greeks, who normally diluted wine with water. -- Megillus's militaristic retort ('we send them flying') exemplifies the very reductionism the Stranger is challenging: judging institutions solely by military outcomes. -- The Stranger's point about cultural relativism ('this is our custom') echoes Herodotus 3.38 on the relativity of burial customs.

narrative:
"Nay, my good friend, do not say that; there have been, as there always will be, flights and pursuits of which no account can be given," replied the Athenian Stranger, "and therefore we cannot say that victory or defeat in battle affords more than a doubtful proof of the goodness or badness of institutions.

"For when the greater states conquer and enslave the lesser, as the Syracusans have done the Locrians, who appear to be the best-governed people in their part of the world, or as the Athenians have done the Ceans -- and there are ten thousand other instances of the same sort of thing -- all this is not to the point; let us endeavour rather to form a conclusion about each institution in itself and say nothing, at present, of victories and defeats. Let us only say that such and such a custom is honourable, and another not. And first permit me to tell you how good and bad are to be estimated in reference to these very matters."

"How do you mean?" asked Megillus.
refashioned:
"Don't say that, my friend," the Stranger replied. "There have always been, and always will be, victories and defeats that prove nothing. We cannot say that success or failure in battle provides more than doubtful evidence of whether institutions are good or bad.

"When larger states conquer and enslave smaller ones -- as Syracuse did to Locri, which appears to be the best-governed state in its region, or as Athens did to Ceos -- and there are ten thousand similar examples -- none of this is relevant. Let us instead try to evaluate each institution on its own merits, setting victories and defeats aside. Let us simply determine which customs are honorable and which are not.

"But first, let me explain how good and bad should be assessed in matters like these."

"What do you mean?" Megillus asked.
elegant: Military victory proves nothing about the quality of institutions: well-governed states are conquered by larger ones all the time. Syracuse enslaved Locri; Athens conquered Ceos. Instead, each institution must be evaluated on its own merits, independent of military outcomes.
summary: The Stranger dismisses military victory as evidence of institutional quality, citing examples of well-governed states conquered by larger powers. He insists institutions must be evaluated on their own merits, not by battlefield results.
distilled: Military victory is unreliable evidence of good institutions -- might does not make right in legislation.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- Locri Epizephyrii in southern Italy was famous for its early written law code (attributed to Zaleucus, c. 660 BCE) and was widely regarded as well-governed. Syracuse, the largest Greek city in Sicily, repeatedly dominated smaller neighbors. -- Ceos was an Aegean island subjugated by Athens during the Delian League period. -- The Stranger's self-criticism is notable: he includes Athens among the unjust conquerors. -- The principle that institutions must be evaluated intrinsically rather than by their military outcomes challenges the entire Cretan-Spartan framework established in 1.4 and anticipates Aristotle's argument in Politics 7.2 that military prowess is not the goal of the state.

narrative:
"All those who are ready at a moment's notice to praise or censure any practice which is matter of discussion, seem to me to proceed in a wrong way," said the Athenian Stranger. "Let me give you an illustration of what I mean. You may suppose a person to be praising wheat as a good kind of food, whereupon another person instantly blames wheat, without ever enquiring into its effect or use, or in what way, or to whom, or with what, or in what state and how, wheat is to be given.

"And that is just what we are doing in this discussion. At the very mention of the word intoxication, one side is ready with their praises and the other with their censures; which is absurd. For either side adduces their witnesses and approvers, and some of us think that we speak with authority because we have many witnesses; and others because they see those who abstain conquering in battle, and this again is disputed by us.

"Now I cannot say that I shall be satisfied, if we go on discussing each of the remaining laws in the same way. And about this very point of intoxication I should like to speak in another way, which I hold to be the right one; for if number is to be the criterion, are there not myriads upon myriads of nations ready to dispute the point with you, who are only two cities?"
refashioned:
"People who instantly praise or condemn any practice the moment it comes up for discussion are going about it the wrong way," said the Stranger. "Here is an illustration. Suppose someone praises wheat as good food, and another person immediately condemns wheat -- without asking about its effects, its uses, who should eat it, how it should be prepared, what it should be combined with, or in what condition it should be consumed.

"That is exactly what we are doing. The moment the word 'intoxication' is mentioned, one side rushes to praise it and the other to condemn it. This is absurd. Each side cites its witnesses and supporters: some of us think we speak with authority because we have many supporters; others because they point to abstainers winning battles -- and even that is disputed.

"I won't be satisfied if we keep discussing laws this way. On this specific matter of intoxication, I want to take a different approach -- the right one, as I see it. If sheer numbers are the standard, there are myriads of nations ready to challenge you, and you are only two cities."
elegant: The Stranger attacks the method of the debate: rushing to praise or condemn a practice without examining its effects, context, and proper use is like praising or blaming wheat as food without asking how, when, and for whom it should be eaten. Each side cites witnesses and military outcomes, but numbers prove nothing -- and two cities cannot outweigh myriads of nations.
summary: The Stranger criticizes the method of discussion: praising or condemning intoxication without examining its proper use is like judging wheat without asking how it should be consumed. He rejects both popular consensus and military outcomes as criteria, proposing a more rigorous approach.
distilled: Judging any practice requires examining its effects, context, and proper use -- not collecting witnesses or counting battlefield victories.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The wheat analogy is simple but effective: it reframes the question from 'is drinking good or bad?' to 'under what conditions, for whom, and in what manner is drinking beneficial or harmful?' -- This methodological point anticipates Aristotle's doctrine of the mean and his emphasis on circumstance (Nicomachean Ethics 2.6-7). -- 'Myriads of nations' drinking vs. two cities abstaining -- the Stranger dismantles Megillus's claim to authority by numbers. -- The insistence on proper method over quick judgment is quintessentially Socratic/Platonic. -- This passage prepares for the analogy of the goatherd (1.34) and the symposiarch (1.37): the question is not whether an institution exists but whether it is properly supervised.

narrative:
"I shall gladly welcome any method of enquiry which is right," said Megillus.

"Let me put the matter thus," said the Athenian Stranger. "Suppose a person to praise the keeping of goats, and the creatures themselves as capital things to have, and then some one who had seen goats feeding without a goatherd in cultivated spots, and doing mischief, were to censure a goat or any other animal who has no keeper, or a bad keeper, would there be any sense or justice in such censure?"

"Certainly not."

"Does a captain require only to have nautical knowledge in order to be a good captain, whether he is sea-sick or not? What do you say?"

"I say that he is not a good captain if, although he have nautical skill, he is liable to sea-sickness."

"And what would you say of the commander of an army? Will he be able to command merely because he has military skill if he be a coward, who, when danger comes, is sick and drunk with fear?"

"Impossible."

"And what if besides being a coward he has no skill?"

"He is a miserable fellow, not fit to be a commander of men, but only of old women."
refashioned:
"I gladly welcome any sound method of inquiry," said Megillus.

"Then consider this," said the Stranger. "Suppose someone praises goat-keeping and goats as wonderful to have. Then someone else, having seen goats wandering unsupervised through cultivated fields and causing damage, condemns goats -- or any other animal without a keeper, or with a bad keeper. Would such criticism make sense?"

"Certainly not."

"Does a captain only need nautical knowledge to be a good captain? What about if he gets seasick?"

"He's not a good captain if he's skilled in seamanship but prone to seasickness."

"And what about an army commander? Can he command effectively with military skill alone if he's a coward who becomes sick and paralyzed with fear at the approach of danger?"

"Impossible."

"And what if he's both a coward and unskilled?"

"Then he's a wretched fellow -- not fit to command men, only old women."
elegant: The Stranger deploys three analogies. Goats are not to blame when they destroy crops without a goatherd -- the fault lies in absent supervision. A captain with knowledge but seasickness is no good captain. A general who is both cowardly and unskilled is worthless. The point: do not blame a practice for its unsupervised effects; blame the absence of proper leadership.
summary: The Stranger uses three analogies -- unsupervised goats, a seasick captain, and a cowardly general -- to establish that practices should not be blamed for their mismanaged effects. The fault lies not in the practice itself but in the absence of competent leadership.
distilled: Do not blame goats for destroying crops when there is no goatherd -- blame the absence of proper supervision.
notes: Speakers: Megillus and the Athenian Stranger. -- The goat analogy is charming and characteristic of Plato's use of everyday images to illuminate philosophical points (cf. the allegory of the cave, the allegory of the charioteer). -- The progression from goatherd to captain to general builds from the mundane to the civic, preparing for the argument that drinking parties need a competent 'commander' -- the symposiarch. -- Megillus's dismissive remark about commanding 'old women' reflects typical Greek gender attitudes. -- The underlying principle -- that leadership determines whether an institution functions well -- is applied throughout the Laws to every area of legislation.

narrative:
"And what would you say of some one who blames or praises any sort of meeting which is intended by nature to have a ruler, and is well enough when under his presidency?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "The critic, however, has never seen the society meeting together at an orderly feast under the control of a president, but always without a ruler or with a bad one -- when observers of this class praise or blame such meetings, are we to suppose that what they say is of any value?"

"Certainly not, if they have never seen or been present at such a meeting when rightly ordered," replied Megillus.

"Reflect; may not banqueters and banquets be said to constitute a kind of meeting?"

"Of course."
refashioned:
"What about someone who praises or blames a type of gathering that is meant to have a leader and works perfectly well under proper leadership?" the Stranger asked. "But this critic has never seen such a gathering conducted as an orderly feast under a competent president -- only without a leader, or under a bad one. Should we take their praise or blame seriously?"

"Certainly not, if they've never seen such a meeting rightly conducted," Megillus agreed.

"Now consider: can we not say that banqueters and banquets constitute a kind of meeting?"

"Of course."
elegant: Anyone who criticizes a type of gathering -- having only seen it badly managed -- cannot be taken seriously. The question then becomes: can a banquet be considered a form of meeting that needs proper leadership? Obviously yes.
summary: The Stranger argues that critics who have only seen gatherings mismanaged have no authority to judge them. He then establishes that banquets are a form of meeting, preparing the argument that drinking parties require competent leadership.
distilled: Those who have only seen drinking parties mismanaged cannot legitimately judge the practice -- the question is whether they have ever seen one properly led.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Megillus. -- This argument applies the goatherd principle directly to symposia (drinking parties). -- The Greek symposion was a formally structured event with a symposiarch (master of the drinking) who regulated the mixing of wine and the pace of consumption. -- Megillus, coming from a culture that banned drinking, has by definition never seen a properly managed symposium -- which the Stranger will use against him. -- The argument structure is: (1) a practice needs leadership, (2) you've only seen it without leadership, (3) therefore your criticism is uninformed.

narrative: "And did any one ever see this sort of convivial meeting rightly ordered?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Of course you two will answer that you have never seen them at all, because they are not customary or lawful in your country; but I have come across many of them in many different places, and moreover I have made enquiries about them wherever I went, as I may say, and never did I see or hear of anything of the kind which was carried on altogether rightly; in some few particulars they might be right, but in general they were utterly wrong."
refashioned: "Has anyone ever actually seen a drinking party rightly conducted?" the Stranger asked. "You two will of course say you've never seen one at all, since they're neither customary nor legal in your countries. I, however, have encountered many of them in many different places, and I've investigated them wherever I traveled. I can say that I never saw or heard of one that was conducted altogether rightly. A few details might be right, but on the whole they were completely wrong."
elegant: Neither Megillus nor Cleinias has ever seen a drinking party -- they are illegal in their countries. The Stranger has seen many across the Greek world, yet even he has never seen one conducted entirely rightly. The ideal drinking party remains unrealized.
summary: The Stranger acknowledges that Megillus and Cleinias have never witnessed a drinking party, and admits that even he, having attended many across the Greek world, has never seen one conducted properly. The ideal remains unrealized.
distilled: No one -- not the abstaining Spartans and Cretans, nor the Stranger himself -- has ever witnessed a properly conducted drinking party.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The Stranger's admission that he has traveled widely and investigated symposia everywhere is autobiographical detail rare in Platonic dialogues. -- That the ideal drinking party has never been realized parallels the Republic's admission that the ideal city may never exist in practice (Republic 9.592a-b). -- The Stranger is building toward a constructive proposal: if drinking parties have never been done right, this is an argument for designing them properly, not for abolishing them. -- The universal failure of existing practice doesn't condemn the practice itself -- it condemns the absence of proper regulation.

narrative:
"What do you mean, Stranger, by this remark? Explain; for we, as you say, from our inexperience in such matters, might very likely not know, even if they came in our way, what was right or wrong in such societies," said Cleinias.

"Likely enough; then let me try to be your instructor. You would acknowledge, would you not, that in all gatherings of mankind, of whatever sort, there ought to be a leader?"

"Certainly I should."

"And we were saying just now, that when men are at war the leader ought to be a brave man?"

"We were."

"The brave man is less likely than the coward to be disturbed by fears?"

"That again is true."

"And if there were a possibility of having a general of an army who was absolutely fearless and imperturbable, should we not by all means appoint him?"

"Assuredly."

"Now, however, we are speaking not of a general who is to command an army, when foe meets foe in time of war, but of one who is to regulate meetings of another sort, when friend meets friend in time of peace."

"True."

"And that sort of meeting, if attended with drunkenness, is apt to be unquiet."

"Certainly; the reverse of quiet."

"In the first place, then, the revellers as well as the soldiers will require a ruler?"

"To be sure; no men more so."

"And we ought, if possible, to provide them with a quiet ruler?"

"Of course."

"And he should be a man who understands society; for his duty is to preserve the friendly feelings which exist among the company at the time, and to increase them for the future by his use of the occasion."

"Very true."

"Must we not appoint a sober man and a wise to be our master of the revels? For if the ruler of drinkers be himself young and drunken, and not over-wise, only by some special good fortune will he be saved from doing some great evil."

"It will be by a singular good fortune that he is saved."
refashioned:
"What do you mean, Stranger? Explain -- since we have no experience with such things, we probably wouldn't recognize what's right or wrong about them even if we encountered them," Cleinias said.

"Fair enough; let me instruct you. You'd agree that in every gathering of people, whatever the kind, there should be a leader?"

"Certainly."

"And we said that in wartime the leader should be a brave man?"

"We did."

"And the brave man is less likely than the coward to be unnerved by fear?"

"True."

"And if we could find a general who was absolutely fearless and unshakable, we'd appoint him without hesitation?"

"Of course."

"But now we're not talking about a general commanding an army where foe meets foe in war. We're talking about someone who regulates a different kind of gathering, where friend meets friend in peace."

"True."

"And such a gathering, when drunkenness is involved, tends to be disorderly."

"Certainly -- the opposite of orderly."

"So revelers, like soldiers, need a ruler?"

"Absolutely -- no one more so."

"And we should provide them, if possible, with a calm ruler?"

"Of course."

"Someone who understands social dynamics, whose duty is to preserve the friendly feelings that already exist among the company and to deepen them through skillful management of the occasion."

"Very true."

"Then shouldn't we appoint a sober and wise man as master of the revels? If the ruler of drinkers is himself young, drunk, and not particularly wise, only extraordinary luck will save him from causing great harm."

"Only extraordinary luck indeed."
elegant: Every gathering needs a leader. In war, the leader must be brave and fearless. At a drinking party -- where friend meets friend in peace -- the leader must be calm, socially skilled, sober, and wise. His task is to preserve and deepen the friendly feelings among the company. A drunken leader of drinkers will inevitably cause harm, just as a cowardly general will lose battles.
summary: Through rapid Socratic questioning, the Stranger establishes that drinking parties need leadership just as armies do. The ideal master of revels must be sober, wise, and socially skilled, tasked with preserving and deepening friendship among the company.
distilled: A drinking party needs a sober, wise leader just as an army needs a brave general -- and for the same reason: unled gatherings produce chaos.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger in Socratic question-and-answer. -- The 'master of the revels' (symposiarch) was a real Greek institution: a designated person who controlled the ratio of wine to water and the pace of drinking. -- 'Where friend meets friend in time of peace' vs. 'where foe meets foe in time of war' -- this elegant parallel makes the structural analogy between military and social leadership. -- The qualities required of the symposiarch (sobriety, wisdom, understanding of social dynamics) make him a figure of the philosopher-ruler in miniature. -- The argument's conclusion -- that a sober person must rule the drinking -- is paradoxical: the abstinent person is the ideal leader of the non-abstinent. This parallels the Republic's argument that the philosopher, who least wants power, should rule.

narrative: "Now suppose such associations to be framed in the best way possible in states, and that some one blames the very fact of their existence -- he may very likely be right," said the Athenian Stranger. "But if he blames a practice which he only sees very much mismanaged, he shows in the first place that he is not aware of the mismanagement, and also not aware that everything done in this way will turn out to be wrong, because done without the superintendence of a sober ruler. Do you not see that a drunken pilot or a drunken ruler of any sort will ruin ship, chariot, army -- anything, in short, of which he has the direction?"
refashioned: "Now suppose such gatherings were organized in the best possible way within a state," the Stranger continued. "Someone who criticizes their very existence might well be right. But someone who criticizes the practice only because he's seen it badly managed reveals two kinds of ignorance: first, he doesn't recognize the mismanagement; second, he doesn't realize that anything done without the oversight of a sober ruler will turn out badly. Surely you can see that a drunken pilot, or a drunken ruler of any kind, will ruin a ship, a chariot, an army -- anything he directs?"
elegant: Criticizing a practice because you've only seen it mismanaged reveals ignorance, not insight. A drunken pilot ruins a ship; a drunken ruler ruins anything he directs. The fault lies in absent supervision, not in the practice itself.
summary: The Stranger argues that criticizing drinking because one has only seen it mismanaged shows ignorance of proper management. A drunken ruler ruins anything he directs -- ship, chariot, army. The problem is never the practice itself but the absence of sober supervision.
distilled: Blaming a practice for its unsupervised failures is like blaming ships for sinking when the pilot is drunk.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The drunken pilot is a memorable image that distills the argument: the practice (sailing, drinking) is not the problem; the leadership is. -- Cf. Republic 6.488a-489a, where Socrates uses the 'ship of state' analogy with a different emphasis (the philosopher as the true navigator). -- The Stranger concedes that someone might legitimately object to drinking parties entirely ('he may very likely be right') -- a careful qualification that keeps the argument honest. -- The argument has the structure: if X fails without good leadership, the conclusion is not 'abolish X' but 'provide good leadership.'

narrative: "The last remark is very true, Stranger; and I see quite clearly the advantage of an army having a good leader -- he will give victory in war to his followers, which is a very great advantage; and so of other things," said Cleinias. "But I do not see any similar advantage which either individuals or states gain from the good management of a feast; and I want you to tell me what great good will be effected, supposing that this drinking ordinance is duly established."
refashioned: "Your last point is certainly true, Stranger," said Cleinias. "I can clearly see the advantage of a good military leader -- he brings victory in war, which is an enormous benefit. The same goes for other fields. But I cannot see any similar advantage that individuals or states gain from a well-managed feast. I want you to tell me: what great good would come from a properly established drinking ordinance?"
elegant: Cleinias sees the value of good leadership in war but challenges the Stranger: what is the great benefit of a well-managed drinking party? What good does it actually produce for individuals or states?
summary: Cleinias accepts that good leadership produces victory in war but demands to know what corresponding benefit a well-managed drinking party would bring to individuals and states.
distilled: What great good does a properly managed drinking party actually produce -- what is its equivalent of military victory?
notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- This is a strong and fair challenge: the Stranger has shown that drinking parties need leadership, but has not yet demonstrated their positive value. -- Cleinias's demand for concrete benefits reflects the practical Cretan-Spartan temperament. -- The answer, developed in 1.40-1.60, is that properly managed drinking serves as an educational tool: it tests and trains character, particularly the virtue of temperance. -- This passage sets up the final major argument of Book 1.

narrative:
"If you mean to ask what great good accrues to the state from the right training of a single youth, or of a single chorus -- when the question is put in that form, we cannot deny that the good is not very great in any particular instance," said the Athenian Stranger. "But if you ask what is the good of education in general, the answer is easy -- that education makes good men, and that good men act nobly, and conquer their enemies in battle, because they are good.

"Education certainly gives victory, although victory sometimes produces forgetfulness of education; for many have grown insolent from victory in war, and this insolence has engendered in them innumerable evils; and many a victory has been and will be suicidal to the victors; but education is never suicidal."
refashioned:
"If you're asking what great good comes from properly training a single young person, or a single chorus -- when the question is framed that way, we must admit the benefit in any single case isn't very large," the Stranger replied. "But if you ask about the good of education in general, the answer is straightforward: education produces good people, and good people act nobly -- and they do conquer their enemies in battle, precisely because they are good.

"Education certainly leads to victory. But victory sometimes produces forgetfulness of education: many have become arrogant from military success, and that arrogance has bred innumerable evils. Many victories have been, and will be, suicidal for the victors. But education is never suicidal."
elegant: The benefit of properly managed drinking is educational. A single instance may seem small, but education in general produces good people, who act nobly and even prevail in battle. Victory can be self-destructive -- many victors have been ruined by the arrogance success breeds. But education is never suicidal.
summary: The Stranger answers that the benefit of well-managed drinking is educational. While each instance may seem small, education as a whole produces good people who act nobly. Crucially, victory can become suicidal through the arrogance it breeds, but education never destroys those who possess it.
distilled: Education makes good people and never destroys them -- unlike military victory, which often breeds the arrogance that destroys the victors.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Education is never suicidal' is one of the most memorable aphorisms in the Laws. -- 'Victory suicidal to the victors' alludes to numerous Greek historical examples: Sparta's victory over Athens in the Peloponnesian War led to Spartan overreach and decline; Athens's own imperial overconfidence led to the Sicilian disaster (415-413 BCE). -- The connection between drinking and education is now explicit: the symposium is an educational institution. -- The argument structure is powerful: (1) you value victory, (2) education produces victory more reliably than military training alone, (3) education is superior because it cannot self-destruct, (4) therefore the educational value of symposia exceeds what you initially suppose.

narrative:
"You seem to imply, my friend, that convivial meetings, when rightly ordered, are an important element of education," said Cleinias.

"Certainly I do," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"And can you show that what you have been saying is true?"

"To be absolutely sure of the truth of matters concerning which there are many opinions, is an attribute of the Gods not given to man, Stranger; but I shall be very happy to tell you what I think, especially as we are now proposing to enter on a discussion concerning laws and constitutions."

"Your opinion, Stranger, about the questions which are now being raised, is precisely what we want to hear."
refashioned:
"You seem to be saying that properly organized drinking parties are an important part of education," Cleinias observed.

"That is exactly what I'm saying," the Stranger confirmed.

"And can you prove it?"

"Absolute certainty about matters where many opinions exist belongs to the gods, not to men. But I'm happy to share my view, especially since we're embarking on a discussion about laws and constitutions."

"Your view on the questions we've raised is precisely what we want to hear."
elegant: Cleinias identifies the Stranger's thesis: properly organized drinking is educational. The Stranger claims certainty belongs to the gods but offers his considered opinion -- which is exactly what they need for their discussion of laws.
summary: Cleinias summarizes the Stranger's claim that well-ordered drinking parties serve education. The Stranger acknowledges that absolute certainty belongs to the gods but offers his opinion as sufficient for their legislative inquiry.
distilled: Well-ordered drinking parties serve education -- and while certainty belongs to the gods, informed opinion suffices for lawmaking.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- 'Absolute certainty is an attribute of the Gods not given to man' is a remarkable epistemological statement, reflecting the humbler tone of the Laws compared to the Republic's bolder claims about philosophical knowledge. -- The distinction between divine certainty and human opinion (doxa) echoes the Timaeus 29b-d, where Timaeus says his cosmological account can only be a 'likely story' (eikos mythos). -- The Stranger's willingness to proceed on the basis of opinion rather than certainty is philosophically significant: legislation requires practical wisdom, not metaphysical knowledge. -- This passage transitions from the preliminary argument to the detailed case for drinking as education.

narrative:
"Very good; I will try to find a way of explaining my meaning, and you shall try to have the gift of understanding me," said the Athenian Stranger. "But first let me make an apology. The Athenian citizen is reputed among all the Hellenes to be a great talker, whereas Sparta is renowned for brevity, and the Cretans have more wit than words.

"Now I am afraid of appearing to elicit a very long discourse out of very small materials. For drinking indeed may appear to be a slight matter, and yet is one which cannot be rightly ordered according to nature, without correct principles of music; these are necessary to any clear or satisfactory treatment of the subject, and music again runs up into education generally, and there is much to be said about all this.

"What would you say then to leaving these matters for the present, and passing on to some other question of law?"
refashioned:
"Good -- I'll try to explain my meaning, and you try to understand me," the Stranger said. "But first, an apology. The Athenian citizen has a reputation among all Greeks for talking at length, while Sparta is famous for brevity, and the Cretans have more wit than words.

"I'm afraid it will seem like I'm spinning an enormous discourse from very small material. Drinking may appear to be a trivial subject, yet it cannot be properly understood according to nature without correct principles of music. These principles are necessary for any clear treatment of the topic, and music in turn leads into education generally -- and there is a great deal to say about all of that.

"Would you prefer to set these matters aside and move on to another question of law?"
elegant: The Stranger apologizes for his Athenian loquacity before his laconic Spartan and witty Cretan companions. He warns that drinking, though seemingly trivial, cannot be properly understood without principles of music, which lead to education generally -- a vast subject. He offers to drop it, testing whether his audience is truly committed.
summary: The Stranger warns that the subject of drinking, though seemingly trivial, connects to music and education in ways that require extensive discussion. He apologizes for his Athenian verbosity and offers to change the subject, testing his companions' commitment.
distilled: Drinking properly understood requires principles of music and education -- the subject is far deeper than it appears.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The stereotypes -- Athenians as talkative, Spartans as laconic, Cretans as witty -- were well-established Greek cultural commonplaces. -- The connection between drinking, music, and education is the intellectual core of Laws Books 1-2. Music (mousike) in Greek meant all the arts of the Muses, including poetry, song, and dance -- not just instrumental music. -- The Stranger's offer to change the subject is a classic rhetorical device: it ensures his listeners actively choose to continue, increasing their engagement. -- The claim that drinking requires music connects to the role of the Dionysiac chorus in Athenian education (developed in Book 2).

narrative:
"O Athenian Stranger, let me tell you what perhaps you do not know, that our family is the proxenus of your state," said Megillus. "I imagine that from their earliest youth all boys, when they are told that they are the proxeni of a particular state, feel kindly towards their second country; and this has certainly been my own feeling.

"I can well remember from the days of my boyhood, how, when any Lacedaemonians praised or blamed the Athenians, they used to say to me -- 'See, Megillus, how ill or how well,' as the case might be, 'has your state treated us'; and having always had to fight your battles against detractors when I heard you assailed, I became warmly attached to you. And I always like to hear the Athenian tongue spoken; the common saying is quite true, that a good Athenian is more than ordinarily good, for he is the only man who is freely and genuinely good by the divine inspiration of his own nature, and is not manufactured.

"Therefore be assured that I shall like to hear you say whatever you have to say."
refashioned:
"Athenian Stranger, let me tell you something you may not know," said Megillus. "My family holds the position of proxenus -- diplomatic representative -- for your state. I imagine that all boys, when they learn from childhood that they are proxeni for a particular state, develop warm feelings toward that second country. That has certainly been my experience.

"I can remember from my boyhood how, whenever Spartans praised or criticized the Athenians, they would say to me: 'Look, Megillus, how well or how badly your state has treated us.' Having always had to defend you against critics, I became genuinely fond of Athens. I enjoy hearing the Athenian way of speaking. And the common saying is quite true: a good Athenian is exceptionally good, because he is the only person who is freely and genuinely virtuous by the divine inspiration of his own nature, not by manufactured training.

"So please be assured -- I want to hear whatever you have to say."
elegant: Megillus reveals he is Athens's proxenus (diplomatic representative) in Sparta and has defended Athens since boyhood. He affirms the saying that a good Athenian is uniquely good -- virtuous by nature and divine inspiration rather than by training. He urges the Stranger to continue without restraint.
summary: Megillus reveals his family serves as Athens's diplomatic representative in Sparta, giving him lifelong affection for Athens. He endorses the saying that good Athenians are uniquely virtuous -- naturally good rather than manufactured -- and enthusiastically invites the Stranger to continue.
distilled: Megillus's personal connection to Athens as proxenus gives him deep appreciation for Athenian discourse and natural virtue.
notes: Speaker: Megillus. -- A proxenus was a citizen of one state who served as the official host and representative of another state's interests -- similar to a modern honorary consul. The position was hereditary. -- 'A good Athenian is more than ordinarily good... freely and genuinely good by divine inspiration' is a remarkable compliment from a Spartan, whose culture emphasized manufactured virtue through the agoge. -- The contrast between 'natural' Athenian goodness and 'manufactured' Spartan virtue echoes a major theme: is virtue produced by nature, training, or divine gift? Cf. Meno 70a on whether virtue is teachable. -- Megillus's warm personal narrative humanizes the dialogue and establishes genuine cross-cultural friendship among the speakers.

narrative:
"Yes, Stranger; and when you have heard me speak, say boldly what is in your thoughts," said Cleinias. "Let me remind you of a tie which unites you to Crete. You must have heard here the story of the prophet Epimenides, who was of my family, and came to Athens ten years before the Persian war, in accordance with the response of the Oracle, and offered certain sacrifices which the God commanded.

"The Athenians were at that time in dread of the Persian invasion; and he said that for ten years they would not come, and that when they came, they would go away again without accomplishing any of their objects, and would suffer more evil than they inflicted. At that time my forefathers formed ties of hospitality with you; thus ancient is the friendship which I and my parents have had for you."
refashioned:
"Yes, Stranger -- and once you've heard me, speak your mind freely," Cleinias added. "Let me remind you of a bond connecting you to Crete. You've surely heard the story of the prophet Epimenides, who was of my family. He came to Athens ten years before the Persian War, following an oracle's instruction, and performed certain sacrifices the god commanded.

"The Athenians were then terrified of the Persian invasion. Epimenides told them the Persians would not come for ten years, and when they did come, they would leave without achieving their objectives and suffer more harm than they caused. It was then that my ancestors established ties of hospitality with your people. So the friendship between my family and Athens is ancient indeed."
elegant: Cleinias reveals his own connection to Athens: the prophet Epimenides, his ancestor, came to Athens before the Persian Wars, performed sacred rites, and prophesied that the Persians would fail. This created bonds of hospitality between Cleinias's family and Athens -- an ancient friendship.
summary: Cleinias reveals that the prophet Epimenides was his ancestor and visited Athens before the Persian Wars to perform sacred rites and deliver prophecy. This established ancient ties of hospitality between his family and Athens, paralleling Megillus's connection.
distilled: Cleinias's family bond with Athens through the prophet Epimenides creates a second tie of friendship, complementing Megillus's proxeny.
notes: Speaker: Cleinias. -- Epimenides of Crete was a semi-legendary sage, purifier, and prophet (c. 600 BCE). He was said to have purified Athens after the Cylonian affair. Aristotle, Diogenes Laertius, and Plutarch all discuss him. -- The claim that Epimenides came 'ten years before the Persian War' places the visit c. 500-490 BCE, later than most ancient accounts suggest (c. 596 BCE). The chronology is debated. -- The ties of hospitality (xenia) between prominent families of different cities were a fundamental institution of Greek interstate relations. -- Both Megillus and Cleinias now have personal bonds with Athens, ensuring the Stranger's critiques are received in a spirit of friendship rather than hostility. -- Together, 1.43 and 1.44 establish that all three speakers have pre-existing relationships of trust -- a crucial dramatic foundation for the frank critique that follows.

narrative:
"You seem to be quite ready to listen; and I am also ready to perform as much as I can of an almost impossible task, which I will nevertheless attempt," said the Athenian Stranger. "At the outset of the discussion, let me define the nature and power of education; for this is the way by which our argument must travel onwards to the God Dionysus."

"Let us proceed, if you please," said Cleinias.

"Well, then, if I tell you what are my notions of education, will you consider whether they satisfy you?"

"Let us hear."
refashioned:
"You seem fully ready to listen, and I am ready to attempt what is nearly an impossible task," said the Stranger. "At the start of this discussion, let me define the nature and power of education -- for this is the path our argument must take on its way to the god Dionysus."

"Let us proceed," said Cleinias.

"If I tell you my views on education, will you judge whether they satisfy you?"

"Let us hear them."
elegant: The Stranger announces he will define education -- the path that leads their argument to Dionysus. The task is nearly impossible, but he will attempt it.
summary: The Stranger announces he will define education as the necessary foundation for their argument about drinking, which leads to the god Dionysus. Both companions are eager to hear his account.
distilled: The argument must pass through a definition of education on its way to understanding Dionysus and the value of wine.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- 'Our argument must travel onwards to the God Dionysus' -- the connection between education and Dionysus (god of wine, theater, and ecstasy) will be developed in Book 2. -- The Stranger's modesty ('an almost impossible task') is characteristic of the Laws' more tentative epistemology compared to the Republic's confident assertions. -- The definition of education that follows (1.46-1.47) is one of the most important passages in all of Plato's works.

narrative:
"According to my view, any one who would be good at anything must practise that thing from his youth upwards, both in sport and earnest, in its several branches," said the Athenian Stranger. "For example, he who is to be a good builder, should play at building children's houses; he who is to be a good husbandman, at tilling the ground; and those who have the care of their education should provide them when young with mimic tools. They should learn beforehand the knowledge which they will afterwards require for their art.

"For example, the future carpenter should learn to measure or apply the line in play; and the future warrior should learn riding, or some other exercise, for amusement, and the teacher should endeavour to direct the children's inclinations and pleasures, by the help of amusements, to their final aim in life. The most important part of education is right training in the nursery. The soul of the child in his play should be guided to the love of that sort of excellence in which when he grows up to manhood he will have to be perfected. Do you agree with me thus far?"

"Certainly," said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"In my view, anyone who wants to excel at anything must practice it from youth onward, both in play and in earnest, across all its branches," the Stranger said. "For example, the future builder should play at constructing toy houses; the future farmer should play at tilling soil. Those responsible for children's education should provide them with miniature tools. They should absorb the knowledge they will later need for their craft.

"The future carpenter should learn measuring and using a line through play. The future soldier should learn riding or similar exercises as amusement. The teacher's task is to direct children's natural inclinations and pleasures, through play, toward their ultimate purpose in life.

"The most important part of education is right training in the nursery. In play, the child's soul should be guided toward loving the kind of excellence in which he will need to be perfected as an adult. Do you agree so far?"

"Certainly," Cleinias replied.
elegant: Education begins in play. The future builder plays at building; the future farmer plays at farming. Teachers must direct children's natural pleasures toward the excellence they will need as adults. The most critical stage is early childhood: guide the child's soul through play to love what is excellent.
summary: The Stranger argues that education must begin in early childhood through play that anticipates adult vocations. Teachers should direct children's natural pleasures toward future excellence. The most important part of education is right training in the nursery.
distilled: Education begins in play: the child's soul must be guided through amusement to love the excellence it will need as an adult.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- 'Right training in the nursery' (orthotrophia) is a key concept. -- The idea that play shapes character anticipates modern developmental psychology and Montessori education. Friedrich Froebel's kindergarten concept echoes these ideas. -- The principle that education should work with children's natural inclinations rather than against them distinguishes Plato's approach from pure Spartan discipline. -- Cf. Republic 7.536d-537a: 'Do not use compulsion, but let early education be a sort of amusement.' -- The 'mimic tools' (toy versions of real implements) anticipate modern educational toys and simulation-based learning. -- The emphasis on early childhood education recurs throughout the Laws (especially Books 7-8).

narrative:
"Then let us not leave the meaning of education ambiguous or ill-defined," said the Athenian Stranger. "At present, when we speak in terms of praise or blame about the bringing-up of each person, we call one man educated and another uneducated, although the uneducated man may be sometimes very well educated for the calling of a retail trader, or of a captain of a ship, and the like.

"For we are not speaking of education in this narrower sense, but of that other education in virtue from youth upwards, which makes a man eagerly pursue the ideal perfection of citizenship, and teaches him how rightly to rule and how to obey. This is the only education which, upon our view, deserves the name; that other sort of training, which aims at the acquisition of wealth or bodily strength, or mere cleverness apart from intelligence and justice, is mean and illiberal, and is not worthy to be called education at all.

"But let us not quarrel with one another about a word, provided that the proposition which has just been granted hold good: to wit, that those who are rightly educated generally become good men. Neither must we cast a slight upon education, which is the first and fairest thing that the best of men can ever have, and which, though liable to take a wrong direction, is capable of reformation. And this work of reformation is the great business of every man while he lives."
refashioned:
"Let us not leave education undefined or ambiguous," the Stranger continued. "People currently praise or blame someone's upbringing, calling one person 'educated' and another 'uneducated' -- even though the 'uneducated' person may be perfectly well trained as a retail trader, a ship captain, or something similar.

"That is not the education we mean. We mean education in virtue from youth onward -- the kind that makes a person eagerly pursue the ideal of citizenship and teaches them both how to rule rightly and how to obey rightly. This alone deserves the name 'education.' Training aimed merely at acquiring wealth, bodily strength, or cleverness without intelligence and justice is base and illiberal, and does not deserve to be called education at all.

"But let's not argue over a word, as long as this principle holds: those who are rightly educated generally become good people. We must not belittle education itself. It is the first and finest thing the best of people can possess. Though it can go in a wrong direction, it is always capable of reform. And this work of reformation is every person's great task throughout life."
elegant: True education is not vocational training for traders or captains. It is education in virtue -- pursuing the perfection of citizenship, learning to rule and to obey rightly. Training aimed only at wealth, strength, or cleverness without justice is not education at all. Education is the finest possession of the best people, always capable of reform -- and reformation is the great business of every person's life.
summary: The Stranger defines true education: not vocational training but education in virtue that makes a person pursue ideal citizenship and learn both to rule and to obey. Training for mere wealth, strength, or cleverness without justice is unworthy of the name. Education is always reformable, and reformation is every person's lifelong task.
distilled: True education is education in virtue -- teaching people to rule and obey rightly -- and its reformation is every person's lifelong work.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is one of the most important definitions of education in Western philosophy. -- 'Education in virtue from youth upwards' (paideia pros areten) -- the Greek concept of paideia encompasses culture, cultivation, and character formation, not just instruction. -- The distinction between true education and vocational training anticipates the liberal arts tradition. -- 'Teaches him how rightly to rule and how to obey' -- this bidirectional definition anticipates Aristotle's famous statement that the good citizen must know how to rule and be ruled (Politics 3.4, 1277a26-27). -- 'Training which aims at the acquisition of wealth or bodily strength, or mere cleverness apart from intelligence and justice, is mean and illiberal' -- 'illiberal' (aneleutheros) literally means 'unfree,' i.e., unworthy of a free person. -- 'Education is the first and fairest thing' -- education as the highest human good is the culmination of the hierarchy of goods established in 1.17. -- 'Capable of reformation... the great business of every man while he lives' -- education is not a one-time event but a lifelong process of self-correction.

narrative:
"Very true; and we entirely agree with you," said Cleinias.

"And we agreed before that they are good men who are able to rule themselves, and bad men who are not," added the Athenian Stranger.

"You are quite right," said Cleinias.

"Let me now proceed, if I can, to clear up the subject a little further by an illustration which I will offer you."

"Proceed."

"Do we not consider each of ourselves to be one?" asked the Stranger.

"We do."

"And each one of us has in his bosom two counsellors, both foolish and also antagonistic; of which we call the one pleasure, and the other pain."

"Exactly."

"Also there are opinions about the future, which have the general name of expectations; and the specific name of fear, when the expectation is of pain; and of hope, when of pleasure; and further, there is reflection about the good or evil of them, and this, when embodied in a decree by the State, is called Law."

"I am hardly able to follow you; proceed, however, as if I were," said Cleinias.

"I am in the like case," added Megillus.
refashioned:
"Very true -- we entirely agree," said Cleinias.

"And we agreed earlier that good people are those who can rule themselves, and bad people are those who cannot," the Stranger added.

"Correct."

"Let me try to clarify this further with an illustration."

"Go on."

"Don't we each consider ourselves to be a single being?"

"We do."

"Yet each of us carries within two counselors -- both foolish and opposed to each other. One we call pleasure, the other pain."

"Exactly."

"We also have expectations about the future. The general term is 'expectation'; the specific term when we expect pain is 'fear'; when we expect pleasure, 'hope.' Beyond these, there is our rational reflection on whether these expectations are good or bad -- and when this reflection is formalized as a public decree, it is called 'Law.'"

"I can barely follow you," Cleinias admitted, "but continue as though I could."

"I'm in the same position," Megillus added.
elegant: Each person is one being, yet contains two opposing inner counselors: pleasure and pain. We also carry expectations -- fear (expecting pain) and hope (expecting pleasure). Our rational judgment about whether these expectations are good or bad, when formalized by the state, becomes Law. Both Cleinias and Megillus confess difficulty following this abstract argument.
summary: The Stranger analyzes the inner life: each person contains two opposing counselors (pleasure and pain), plus expectations about the future (fear and hope), plus rational reflection on their value. When this reflection is embodied in a state decree, it becomes Law. Both interlocutors find this hard to follow.
distilled: Law is the state's embodiment of rational reflection on pleasure, pain, fear, and hope -- the inner forces that govern human behavior.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, the Athenian Stranger, and Megillus. -- The two 'counselors' (pleasure and pain) prefigure the puppet analogy in 1.49. -- The definition of law as rational reflection embodied in a state decree is highly original. It makes law an externalization of reason's governance over passion. -- The progression from internal psychology (pleasure/pain/fear/hope) to public law is characteristic of Plato's method of deriving political structures from the soul's structure (cf. Republic 2.368d). -- Cleinias's and Megillus's honest admission of difficulty is refreshing: even intelligent, experienced men find this level of abstraction challenging. -- The identification of fear with expectation of pain and hope with expectation of pleasure anticipates Stoic definitions of the passions (cf. Chrysippus in Diogenes Laertius 7.110-116).

narrative:
"Let us look at the matter thus," said the Athenian Stranger. "May we not conceive each of us living beings to be a puppet of the Gods, either their plaything only, or created with a purpose -- which of the two we cannot certainly know? But we do know, that these affections in us are like cords and strings, which pull us different and opposite ways, and to opposite actions; and herein lies the difference between virtue and vice.

"According to the argument there is one among these cords which every man ought to grasp and never let go, but to pull with it against all the rest; and this is the sacred and golden cord of reason, called by us the common law of the State; there are others which are hard and of iron, but this one is soft because golden; and there are several other kinds.

"Now we ought always to cooperate with the lead of the best, which is law. For inasmuch as reason is beautiful and gentle, and not violent, her rule must needs have ministers in order to help the golden principle in vanquishing the other principles.
