And thus the moral of the tale about our being puppets will not have been lost, and the meaning of the expression 'superior or inferior to a man's self' will become clearer; and the individual, attaining to right reason in this matter of pulling the strings of the puppet, should live according to its rule; while the city, receiving the same from some god or from one who has knowledge of these things, should embody it in a law, to be her guide in her dealings with herself and with other states.

"In this way virtue and vice will be more clearly distinguished by us. And when they have become clearer, education and other institutions will in like manner become clearer; and in particular that question of convivial entertainment, which may seem, perhaps, to have been a very trifling matter, and to have taken a great many more words than were necessary."
refashioned:
"Consider it this way," said the Stranger. "We can think of each living being as a puppet of the gods -- either merely their plaything or created with a purpose; we cannot know which for certain. But we do know that the feelings within us are like cords and strings pulling us in different, opposite directions toward opposing actions. This is where the difference between virtue and vice lies.

"The argument tells us there is one cord among these that every person should grasp and never release, pulling with it against all the rest: the sacred and golden cord of reason, which we call the common law of the state. The other cords are hard and iron; this one is soft because it is golden.

"We must always cooperate with the guidance of the best, which is law. Since reason is beautiful and gentle, not violent, her rule needs servants to help the golden principle overcome the others.

"This is the moral of the puppet metaphor. It also clarifies what it means to be 'superior or inferior to oneself.' The individual who grasps right reason and lives by its guidance does well. The city that receives this guidance -- from some god or from someone who understands these things -- should embody it in law to guide its dealings with itself and other states.

"Once virtue and vice become clearer, education and other institutions will become clearer too -- including the question of convivial entertainment, which may seem trivial and to have consumed far more words than necessary."
elegant: We are puppets of the gods, pulled by opposing cords -- pleasure, pain, fear, hope. Among these is one sacred golden cord: reason, identical with law. Though golden, it is soft and gentle, not violent, so it needs assistance to overcome the hard iron cords of passion. The individual who holds fast to reason is 'superior to himself'; the city that embodies reason in law governs well. This puppet metaphor illuminates virtue, vice, education, and even the seemingly trivial question of drinking parties.
summary: The Stranger presents the famous puppet metaphor: humans are pulled by opposing cords of passion, but the golden cord of reason -- identified with law -- must be held fast against the rest. Reason is gentle and needs support to prevail. This clarifies self-mastery, the nature of law, education, and even the question of drinking.
distilled: We are puppets pulled by iron cords of passion, but must hold fast to the soft golden cord of reason -- which is law.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The puppet (thauma) metaphor is one of Plato's most original images, unique to the Laws. -- The golden cord of reason vs. iron cords of passion creates a powerful material metaphor for the soul's inner conflict. Gold is precious but soft; iron is common but hard. Reason requires institutional support because it cannot overpower passion through force alone. -- 'Whether their plaything only, or created with a purpose' -- this theological uncertainty is remarkable and distinctive of the Laws' more cautious theology. -- The identification of individual reason with 'the common law of the State' makes law the social expression of individual rationality -- a profound philosophical claim. -- 'Reason is beautiful and gentle, and not violent' -- this characterization of reason as non-coercive anticipates Habermas's discourse ethics. -- The passage ties together all the threads of Book 1: the war within the self (1.6), self-mastery (1.6-1.7), the hierarchy of goods (1.17), and education (1.46-1.47).

narrative:
"Perhaps, however, the theme may turn out not to be unworthy of the length of discourse," said Cleinias.

"Very good; let us proceed with any enquiry which really bears on our present object," replied the Athenian Stranger.

"Proceed."

"Suppose that we give this puppet of ours drink -- what will be the effect on him?"

"Having what in view do you ask that question?"

"Nothing as yet; but I ask generally, when the puppet is brought to the drink, what sort of result is likely to follow. I will endeavour to explain my meaning more clearly: what I am now asking is this -- Does the drinking of wine heighten and increase pleasures and pains, and passions and loves?"

"Very greatly."

"And are perception and memory, and opinion and prudence, heightened and increased? Do not these qualities entirely desert a man if he becomes saturated with drink?"

"Yes, they entirely desert him."

"Does he not return to the state of soul in which he was when a young child?"

"He does."

"Then at that time he will have the least control over himself?"

"The least."

"And will he not be in a most wretched plight?"

"Most wretched."

"Then not only an old man but also a drunkard becomes a second time a child?"

"Well said, Stranger."

"Is there any argument which will prove to us that we ought to encourage the taste for drinking instead of doing all we can to avoid it?"

"I suppose that there is; you at any rate, were just now saying that you were ready to maintain such a doctrine."

"True, I was; and I am ready still, seeing that you have both declared that you are anxious to hear me."

"To be sure we are, if only for the strangeness of the paradox, which asserts that a man ought of his own accord to plunge into utter degradation."

"Are you speaking of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And what would you say about the body, my friend? Are you not surprised at any one of his own accord bringing upon himself deformity, leanness, ugliness, decrepitude?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"Perhaps the topic will prove worthy of all these words," Cleinias observed.

"Good -- let us continue with whatever truly bears on our subject," the Stranger replied.

"Go on."

"What happens if we give our puppet drink?"

"What are you getting at?"

"Nothing specific yet. I'm asking generally: when the puppet is brought to wine, what results? Let me put it more clearly: does drinking wine intensify pleasures, pains, passions, and desires?"

"Enormously."

"And what about perception, memory, judgment, and prudence? Don't these abandon a person entirely when he's saturated with drink?"

"They desert him completely."

"Doesn't he return to the state of soul he was in as a young child?"

"He does."

"And at that point he has the least control over himself?"

"The very least."

"And isn't he in the most wretched condition?"

"The most wretched."

"So not only an old man, but also a drunkard, becomes a child for the second time?"

"Well said, Stranger."

"Is there any argument that could convince us to encourage drinking rather than avoid it entirely?"

"There must be -- you were just claiming you were ready to make that argument."

"I was, and I still am, since you've both said you want to hear it."

"We certainly do -- if only because of the sheer strangeness of the paradox: that a person should voluntarily plunge into utter degradation."

"Are you speaking of degradation of the soul?"

"Yes."

"And what about the body? Aren't you equally surprised when someone voluntarily brings upon himself deformity, weakness, ugliness, and decrepitude?"

"Certainly."
elegant: Wine intensifies pleasures, pains, and passions while driving out perception, memory, and prudence. The drunkard reverts to the soul of a child -- minimal self-control, maximum wretchedness. Yet the Stranger maintains there is an argument for encouraging this voluntary degradation. The paradox seems as strange for the soul as it would for someone who voluntarily brought physical deformity upon himself.
summary: The Stranger applies the puppet metaphor to drinking: wine amplifies passions and desires while expelling perception, memory, and prudence, reducing the drinker to a childlike state with minimal self-control. He then poses the paradox: why should anyone voluntarily undergo this degradation?
distilled: Wine intensifies passions and strips away reason, reducing the drinker to a child -- yet there is an argument for this voluntary degradation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger in rapid dialogue. -- 'A drunkard becomes a second time a child' is a vivid formulation. The regression to childhood echoes the puppet metaphor: when the golden cord of reason is loosened, only the iron cords of passion remain. -- The analogy between voluntary soul-degradation and voluntary physical degradation sets up the medical analogy in 1.51: just as one might voluntarily submit to pain for the sake of health, one might submit to the degradation of drink for the sake of self-knowledge. -- The 'paradox' (paradoxos = contrary to expectation) that the Stranger will argue for -- that controlled exposure to degradation serves education -- is genuinely counterintuitive and philosophically original. -- The interlocutors' eagerness to hear the paradox resolved shows Plato's dramatic skill in building intellectual suspense.

narrative: "Yet when a man goes of his own accord to a doctor's shop, and takes medicine, is he not aware that soon, and for many days afterwards, he will be in a state of body which he would die rather than accept as the permanent condition of his life?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Are not those who train in gymnasia, at first beginning reduced to a state of weakness?"
refashioned: "Yet when a person voluntarily goes to a doctor and takes medicine," the Stranger asked, "isn't he fully aware that soon, and for many days afterward, he'll be in a physical state he would rather die than accept permanently? And don't those who begin training in gymnasia start out in a state of weakness?"
elegant: People voluntarily submit to medicine that makes them miserable and to training that weakens them -- because the temporary degradation serves a greater purpose. The same logic applies to drinking.
summary: The Stranger draws an analogy: people voluntarily take medicine that makes them miserable and begin exercise regimens that initially weaken them. Temporary physical degradation is accepted for the sake of long-term improvement.
distilled: We voluntarily submit to medicine and training that temporarily degrade us -- why not to drinking that serves a similar educational purpose?
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The medical analogy is a favorite Platonic device (cf. Gorgias 478a-b, 521e-522a; Republic 3.405c-408e). -- The gymnasium analogy is especially apt given the context: Sparta's gymnasia cause initial weakness for the sake of later strength, just as the Stranger's proposed drinking regime causes initial loss of reason for the sake of later temperance. -- The argument structure is: (1) you accept temporary physical degradation for long-term physical health, (2) you should therefore accept temporary psychological degradation for long-term moral health. -- This is the key move that resolves the paradox posed in 1.50.

narrative:
"Yes, all that is well known," said Cleinias.

"Also that they go of their own accord for the sake of the subsequent benefit?" asked the Athenian Stranger.

"Very good."

"And we may conceive this to be true in the same way of other practices?"

"Certainly."

"And the same view may be taken of the pastime of drinking wine, if we are right in supposing that the same good effect follows?"

"To be sure."

"If such convivialities should turn out to have any advantage equal in importance to that of gymnastic, they are in their very nature to be preferred to mere bodily exercise, inasmuch as they have no accompaniment of pain."

"True; but I hardly think that we shall be able to discover any such benefits to be derived from them."

"That is just what we must endeavour to show. And let me ask you a question: Do we not distinguish two kinds of fear, which are very different?"

"What are they?"

"There is the fear of expected evil."

"Yes."

"And there is the fear of an evil reputation; we are afraid of being thought evil, because we do or say some dishonourable thing, which fear we and all men term shame."

"Certainly."

"These are the two fears, as I called them; one of which is the opposite of pain and other fears, and the opposite also of the greatest and most numerous sort of pleasures."

"Very true."

"And does not the legislator and every one who is good for anything, hold this fear in the greatest honour? This is what he terms reverence, and the confidence which is the reverse of this he terms insolence; and the latter he always deems to be a very great evil both to individuals and to states."

"True."

"Does not this kind of fear preserve us in many important ways? What is there which so surely gives victory and safety in war? For there are two things which give victory -- confidence before enemies, and fear of disgrace before friends."

"There are."

"Then each of us should be fearless and also fearful; and why we should be either has now been determined."

"Certainly."

"And when we want to make any one fearless, we and the law bring him face to face with many fears."

"Clearly."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's well known," said Cleinias.

"And people undergo these voluntarily for the sake of the benefit that follows?"

"Exactly."

"Can't the same principle apply to other practices?"

"Certainly."

"Then the same view could be taken of drinking wine, if the same kind of benefit follows?"

"Of course."

"If drinking parties prove to have advantages comparable to gymnastics, they would actually be preferable to physical exercise, since they involve no pain."

"True -- but I doubt we can find such benefits in drinking."

"That's precisely what we must try to show. Let me ask you: don't we distinguish two very different kinds of fear?"

"What kinds?"

"First, the fear of expected harm."

"Yes."

"Second, the fear of a bad reputation -- the dread of being thought base because we do or say something dishonorable. Everyone calls this shame."

"Certainly."

"These are the two fears. One is the opposite of pain and ordinary fears; it is also the opposite of the greatest and most common pleasures."

"Very true."

"Doesn't the legislator, and everyone of any worth, hold this second fear -- shame -- in the highest honor? He calls it reverence. And the confidence that is its opposite he calls insolence -- which he considers a great evil for both individuals and states."

"True."

"Doesn't this kind of fear -- reverence -- protect us in many crucial ways? What gives victory and safety in war more reliably? Two things: confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends."

"Exactly."

"So each of us should be both fearless and fearful -- and we've now established why."

"Yes."

"And when we want to make someone fearless, we and the law expose them to many fears."

"Clearly."
elegant: If drinking provides benefits comparable to gymnastics -- and without pain -- it is actually preferable. Two kinds of fear exist: fear of harm, and shame (fear of disgrace). The legislator honors shame as 'reverence' and condemns its absence as 'insolence.' Reverence preserves us: victory in war requires both confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends. We must be both fearless and fearful. To make someone fearless, the law exposes them to fears.
summary: The Stranger argues that drinking could be preferable to gymnastics if it provides comparable benefits without pain. He then distinguishes two kinds of fear: fear of harm and shame (fear of disgrace). Shame, which the legislator calls reverence, is essential for both individual virtue and military victory. To train fearlessness, the law exposes people to fears.
distilled: Shame -- the fear of disgrace -- is the legislator's most honored tool, called reverence; and just as fearlessness is trained through exposure to fear, temperance must be trained through exposure to temptation.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The distinction between fear of harm and fear of disgrace (shame/aidos) is philosophically important. Aidos was a fundamental Greek ethical concept -- the internalized sense of honor that restrains behavior. -- 'Reverence' (aidos/aischune) vs. 'insolence' (thrasos/hybris) maps onto the Greek honor/shame culture. -- 'Confidence before enemies and fear of disgrace before friends' captures the dual psychology of military courage: you fight because you fear shame more than death. -- The logical structure is building toward the conclusion: if we train fearlessness by exposure to fear, we should train temperance by exposure to pleasure. -- The claim that drinking is 'painless gymnastics' is both clever and substantive: it suggests that moral training need not be painful to be effective.

narrative: "And when we want to make him rightly fearful, must we not introduce him to shameless pleasures, and train him to take up arms against them, and to overcome them?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "Or does this principle apply to courage only, and must he who would be perfect in valour fight against and overcome his own natural character -- since if he be unpractised and inexperienced in such conflicts, he will not be half the man which he might have been -- and are we to suppose, that with temperance it is otherwise, and that he who has never fought with the shameless and unrighteous temptations of his pleasures and lusts, and conquered them, in earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act, will still be perfectly temperate?"
refashioned: "And when we want to make someone rightly fearful -- meaning reverent -- must we not expose them to shameless pleasures and train them to fight and overcome those pleasures?" the Stranger asked. "Or does this principle of training through exposure apply only to courage? Must the person who wants to be perfectly brave fight against and master his own nature -- since without practice in such struggles he'll be only half the person he could be -- while we somehow assume that temperance works differently? That someone who has never battled the shameless temptations of pleasure and desire, and conquered them in earnest and in play, through word, deed, and action, will still be perfectly temperate?"
elegant: The parallel is complete: just as fearlessness is trained by facing fears, temperance must be trained by facing pleasures. A person who has never fought against shameless temptations -- in earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act -- will never be truly temperate, just as the untested soldier will never be truly brave.
summary: The Stranger completes the parallel: if courage requires training through exposure to fear, temperance requires training through exposure to pleasure. No one who has never struggled against temptation in earnest and in play can be perfectly temperate.
distilled: Temperance, like courage, requires practice against the very thing it must overcome -- one cannot be temperate without having fought temptation.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- This is the culminating argument of Book 1's case for regulated drinking. The logical structure is: (a) courage is trained by exposure to fear, (b) therefore temperance should be trained by exposure to pleasure, (c) regulated drinking provides such exposure. -- 'In earnest and in play, by word, deed, and act' -- this comprehensive formulation echoes 1.46 on education through play and earnest. -- The phrase 'he will not be half the man which he might have been' emphasizes that avoidance stunts moral development rather than protecting it. -- Cf. Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics 2.2 (1104a-b): 'We become temperate by abstaining from pleasures' but also 'we become just by doing just acts, brave by doing brave acts' -- the Aristotelian doctrine of habituation through practice. -- The argument directly refutes the Spartan prohibitionist approach (1.30): mere avoidance produces incomplete virtue.

narrative:
"A most unlikely supposition," agreed Cleinias.

"Suppose that some God had given a fear-potion to men," said the Athenian Stranger, "and that the more a man drank of this the more he regarded himself at every draught as a child of misfortune, and that he feared everything happening or about to happen to him; and that at last the most courageous of men utterly lost his presence of mind for a time, and only came to himself again when he had slept off the influence of the draught."

"But has such a draught, Stranger, ever really been known among men?" asked Cleinias.

"No; but, if there had been, might not such a draught have been of use to the legislator as a test of courage? Might we not go and say to him, 'O legislator, whether you are legislating for the Cretan, or for any other state, would you not like to have a touchstone of the courage and cowardice of your citizens?'"

"'I should,' will be the answer of every one."

"'And you would rather have a touchstone in which there is no risk and no great danger than the reverse?'"

"In that proposition every one may safely agree."
refashioned:
"That's a most unlikely assumption," Cleinias agreed.

"Now suppose," said the Stranger, "that some god had given humanity a fear-potion. The more you drank, the more miserable and terrified you became. You feared everything present and future. Eventually even the bravest person would completely lose his nerve -- recovering only after sleeping off the potion's effects."

"Has such a potion ever actually existed?" Cleinias asked.

"No. But if it had, wouldn't it be useful to the legislator as a test of courage? We could say to him: 'Legislator, whether you're making laws for Crete or any other state, wouldn't you like a touchstone for testing your citizens' courage and cowardice?'

"Every legislator would answer: 'Yes.'

"'And you'd prefer a touchstone that involves no real risk or danger?'

"Everyone would agree to that."
elegant: Imagine a fear-potion that makes even the bravest person lose all courage temporarily. Such a potion would be invaluable to a legislator as a risk-free test of character -- a touchstone for measuring courage and cowardice without anyone getting hurt.
summary: The Stranger proposes a thought experiment: a hypothetical fear-potion that temporarily destroys courage would be invaluable to any legislator as a safe, risk-free test of character. This sets up the argument that wine serves as the real-world equivalent for testing temperance.
distilled: A hypothetical fear-potion would be invaluable as a risk-free test of courage -- and wine is the real equivalent for testing temperance.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- The thought experiment of the fear-potion is a brilliant philosophical device. It establishes the principle that controlled exposure to a negative state can be valuable for assessment and training, without real-world risk. -- 'Touchstone' (basanos) was literally a stone used to test the purity of gold by rubbing it against the stone. The metaphor was common in Greek for any test of character. -- The argument's structure: (1) a hypothetical fear-potion would be useful for testing courage, (2) wine is a real substance that has the opposite effect (inducing overconfidence rather than fear), (3) therefore wine can serve as a real touchstone for testing temperance. -- The fear-potion is imaginary; wine is real. This is the Stranger's rhetorical coup: he will argue that wine is the actual, available tool that his hypothetical illustrates.

narrative:
"'And in order to make use of the draught, you would lead them amid these imaginary terrors, and prove them, when the affection of fear was working upon them, and compel them to be fearless, exhorting and admonishing them; and also honouring them, but dishonouring any one who will not be persuaded by you to be in all respects such as you command him; and if he underwent the trial well and manfully, you would let him go unscathed; but if ill, you would inflict a punishment upon him? Or would you abstain from using the potion altogether, although you have no reason for abstaining?'" continued the Athenian Stranger.

"He would be certain, Stranger, to use the potion," said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"'To use the potion,' the Stranger continued, 'you would lead your citizens through these imaginary terrors, testing them while fear gripped them. You would compel them to be fearless, exhorting and coaching them. You would honor those who responded well, and dishonor anyone who refused to meet your standards. Those who passed the trial manfully, you would release unscathed. Those who failed, you would punish. Or would you refuse to use the potion at all, even though there is no reason to abstain?'"

"The legislator would certainly use the potion," Cleinias confirmed.
elegant: The fear-potion would be used to test citizens under controlled conditions: those who mastered their fear would be honored; those who failed would be punished. No legislator would refuse such a tool without reason. The same logic applies to wine as a test of temperance.
summary: The Stranger describes how the fear-potion would be used: citizens would be tested under its influence, honored for courage, and punished for cowardice. No rational legislator would refuse such a tool. Cleinias agrees.
distilled: Any rational legislator would use a risk-free test of character rather than abstain without reason -- and wine provides exactly such a test for temperance.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- The testing protocol described (controlled exposure, exhortation, honor/dishonor) mirrors Spartan training methods -- but applied to temperance rather than courage. -- 'You have no reason for abstaining' is pointed at the Spartan/Cretan prohibition of drinking: they abstain from using this testing tool without adequate justification. -- The argument is now almost complete: the fear-potion analogy has established that controlled testing of character is valuable; the next step identifies wine as the real-world equivalent.

narrative: "This would be a mode of testing and training which would be wonderfully easy in comparison with those now in use, and might be applied to a single person, or to a few, or indeed to any number," said the Athenian Stranger, "and he would do well who provided himself with the potion only, rather than with any number of other things, whether he preferred to be by himself in solitude, and there contend with his fears, because he was ashamed to be seen by the eye of man until he was perfect; or trusting to the force of his own nature and habits, and believing that he had been already disciplined sufficiently, he did not hesitate to train himself in company with any number of others, and display his power in conquering the irresistible change effected by the draught -- his virtue being such, that he never in any instance fell into any great unseemliness, but was always himself, and left off before he arrived at the last cup, fearing that he, like all other men, might be overcome by the potion."
refashioned:
"This method of testing and training would be wonderfully easy compared to current methods," the Stranger said. "It could be applied to one person, a few, or any number. A person would do well to use the potion alone rather than rely on many other tools -- whether he preferred solitary practice, contending privately with his fears out of shame at being seen until he perfected himself, or whether, trusting his nature and training and believing himself already sufficiently disciplined, he trained openly among others, demonstrating his ability to overcome the potion's irresistible effects.

"The truly virtuous person would never fall into great unseemliness, always remaining himself, and would stop before reaching the last cup -- knowing that, like all people, he might ultimately be overcome."
elegant: This testing method is simpler and more scalable than current approaches. It can be practiced alone (for the modest) or in groups (for the confident). The truly virtuous person maintains control under the potion's influence, never falls into great disgrace, and knows when to stop -- recognizing that even the best can be overcome.
summary: The Stranger praises this testing method as easy, scalable, and adaptable to any circumstance. The virtuous person maintains self-control under the potion's influence and knows when to stop, recognizing that everyone has limits.
distilled: The wise person trains against temptation, maintains composure under its influence, and knows when to stop -- recognizing that even the best have limits.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- 'Left off before he arrived at the last cup' is practical advice embedded in philosophical argument: the wise drinker knows his limits. -- The two modes of training -- private (out of shame) and public (out of confidence) -- map onto different stages of moral development. -- 'He never in any instance fell into any great unseemliness, but was always himself' -- the goal is not to be unaffected by the potion/wine but to maintain one's essential character under its influence. This is a realistic standard of self-mastery: not invulnerability but resilience. -- 'Fearing that he, like all other men, might be overcome' -- the recognition of universal human fallibility is a mark of true wisdom. Even the Stranger does not claim exemption from human weakness.

narrative:
"Yes, Stranger, in that last case, too, he might equally show his self-control," agreed Cleinias.

"Let us return to the lawgiver, and say to him," said the Athenian Stranger, "'Well, lawgiver, there is certainly no such fear-potion which man has either received from the Gods or himself discovered; for witchcraft has no place at our board. But is there any potion which might serve as a test of overboldness and excessive and indiscreet boasting?'"

"I suppose that he will say, Yes -- meaning that wine is such a potion," said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Yes, in that case too he would demonstrate self-control," Cleinias agreed.

"Let us return to the lawgiver," the Stranger said. "We would say to him: 'Lawgiver, no fear-potion has ever been received from the gods or discovered by men -- there is no place for witchcraft at our table. But is there a potion that could serve as a test of overboldness, excessive confidence, and indiscreet boasting?'"

"I suppose the lawgiver would answer: 'Yes' -- meaning wine," said Cleinias.
elegant: The fear-potion is hypothetical -- it does not exist. But wine does exist, and it is the real-world potion that tests overboldness, excessive confidence, and indiscreet boasting. Cleinias himself draws the conclusion.
summary: The Stranger reveals the analogy's payoff: while no fear-potion exists, wine is the real substance that tests overboldness and excessive confidence. Cleinias himself identifies wine as the answer.
distilled: No fear-potion exists, but wine does -- and it is the real test of overboldness, overconfidence, and shamelessness.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and the Athenian Stranger. -- This is the argumentative climax: the long hypothetical about the fear-potion resolves into the claim that wine is its real-world counterpart. -- The fear-potion tested courage (resistance to fear); wine tests temperance (resistance to shameless pleasure and overconfidence). They are complementary. -- 'Witchcraft has no place at our board' -- a pragmatic disclaimer separating philosophy from magic. -- That Cleinias draws the conclusion himself ('I suppose he will say Yes -- meaning wine') is a mark of successful Socratic education: the learner arrives at the insight independently. -- The word translated 'overboldness' (thrasos) was identified earlier (1.52) as the opposite of reverence -- the vice that the legislator most deplores.

narrative: "Is not the effect of this quite the opposite of the effect of the other?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "When a man drinks wine he begins to be better pleased with himself, and the more he drinks the more he is filled full of brave hopes, and conceit of his power, and at last the string of his tongue is loosened, and fancying himself wise, he is brimming over with lawlessness, and has no more fear or respect, and is ready to do or say anything."
refashioned: "Isn't wine's effect exactly the opposite of the fear-potion's?" the Stranger asked. "When a person drinks wine, they begin to feel more pleased with themselves. The more they drink, the more they fill up with brave hopes and inflated confidence. Eventually their tongue is loosened, and imagining themselves wise, they overflow with lawlessness -- losing all fear and respect, ready to do or say anything."
elegant: Wine produces the opposite of fear: increasing self-satisfaction, inflated confidence, loosened tongues, and the illusion of wisdom. At its peak, it produces total lawlessness -- the loss of all shame and restraint.
summary: The Stranger describes wine's progressive effects: growing self-satisfaction, inflated confidence, loosened speech, false wisdom, and ultimately complete lawlessness -- the loss of all fear and respect. This is the exact opposite of the fear-potion.
distilled: Wine progressively inflates confidence, loosens the tongue, and dissolves all shame and restraint -- the perfect opposite of fear.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. -- The progression described (self-satisfaction > confidence > loosened tongue > false wisdom > lawlessness) is a remarkably accurate phenomenology of intoxication. -- 'Fancying himself wise' connects to the Socratic insight that false wisdom is the greatest ignorance (Apology 21d-22e). -- 'Brimming over with lawlessness' (anomia) is the key term: wine dissolves nomos (law/custom), which is why it serves as a test of how deeply law has been internalized. -- The fear-potion strips away courage; wine strips away restraint. Together they would test the full spectrum of virtue. Since only wine exists, the legislator must use it. -- 'Ready to do or say anything' -- this is the condition of maximum vulnerability to vice, and therefore the condition in which character is most fully revealed.

narrative:
"I think that every one will admit the truth of your description," said Cleinias.

"Certainly," added Megillus.

"Now, let us remember, as we were saying, that there are two things which should be cultivated in the soul: first, the greatest courage; secondly, the greatest fear --" said the Athenian Stranger.

"Which you said to be characteristic of reverence, if I am not mistaken," interjected Cleinias.

"Thank you for reminding me. But now, as the habit of courage and fearlessness is to be trained amid fears, let us consider whether the opposite quality is not also to be trained among opposites."

"That is probably the case," agreed Megillus.

"There are times and seasons at which we are by nature more than commonly valiant and bold; now we ought to train ourselves on these occasions to be as free from impudence and shamelessness as possible, and to be afraid to say or suffer or do anything that is base."

"True," said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Everyone will recognize the truth of that description," said Cleinias.

"Certainly," Megillus agreed.

"Now let us recall what we established: two things must be cultivated in the soul -- first, the greatest courage; second, the greatest fear," the Stranger continued.

"Which you said was characteristic of reverence, if I remember correctly," Cleinias interjected.

"Thank you for the reminder. Now, if courage and fearlessness are trained through exposure to fears, shouldn't the opposite quality be trained through exposure to its opposite?"

"That seems likely," Megillus agreed.

"There are natural moments when we feel bolder and more daring than usual. On these occasions, we should train ourselves to remain as free from impudence and shamelessness as possible -- to fear saying, suffering, or doing anything base."

"True," said Cleinias.
elegant: Two things must be cultivated: courage (trained through exposure to fear) and reverence (trained through exposure to boldness-inducing situations). When we naturally feel most daring, we should practice maintaining shame and avoiding baseness. Wine creates exactly such conditions.
summary: The Stranger recalls the twin requirements -- courage and reverence -- and completes the parallel: if courage is trained through fears, reverence should be trained through situations that induce boldness. Those naturally bold moments are when we must practice maintaining restraint and avoiding baseness.
distilled: If courage is trained through exposure to fear, then reverence must be trained through exposure to boldness -- and wine provides that exposure.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Megillus, and the Athenian Stranger. -- Cleinias's reminder that the 'greatest fear' is reverence shows he has been following the argument carefully and internalized the key distinction from 1.52. -- The principle of 'training among opposites' is now fully symmetrical: fear trains courage, boldness-inducing situations train reverence/temperance. -- 'Times and seasons at which we are by nature more than commonly valiant and bold' -- this describes not only drunkenness but also youth, victory, wealth, and any condition that inflates confidence. -- The argument has circled back to its starting point with full justification: regulated drinking is an institution for training temperance, just as gymnasia and the Crypteia are institutions for training courage.

narrative:
"Are not the moments in which we are apt to be bold and shameless such as these?" asked the Athenian Stranger. "When we are under the influence of anger, love, pride, ignorance, avarice, cowardice? or when wealth, beauty, strength, and all the intoxicating workings of pleasure madden us?

"What is better adapted than the festive use of wine, in the first place to test, and in the second place to train the character of a man, if care be taken in the use of it? What is there cheaper, or more innocent? For do but consider which is the greater risk: Would you rather test a man of a morose and savage nature, which is the source of ten thousand acts of injustice, by making bargains with him at a risk to yourself, or by having him as a companion at the festival of Dionysus?

"Or would you, if you wanted to apply a touchstone to a man who is prone to love, entrust your wife, or your sons, or daughters to him, perilling your dearest interests in order to have a view of the condition of his soul?

"I might mention numberless cases, in which the advantage would be manifest of getting to know a character in sport, and without paying dearly for experience. And I do not believe that either a Cretan, or any other man, will doubt that such a test is a fair test, and safer, cheaper, and speedier than any other."

"That is certainly true," agreed Cleinias.

"And this knowledge of the natures and habits of men's souls will be of the greatest use in that art which has the management of them; and that art, if I am not mistaken, is politics."

"Exactly so."
refashioned:
"Aren't these the moments when we tend to be bold and shameless?" the Stranger asked. "When we're under the influence of anger, love, pride, ignorance, greed, or cowardice? Or when wealth, beauty, strength, and all the intoxicating effects of pleasure drive us to excess?

"What is better suited than the festive use of wine to first test and then train a person's character, provided it is used with care? What method is cheaper or more harmless?

"Consider the alternatives. Would you rather evaluate a person with a savage, morose nature -- the source of countless injustices -- by entering into business dealings with him at your own risk? Or by having him as a companion at a festival of Dionysus?

"If you wanted to test a man prone to sexual passion, would you entrust your wife, sons, or daughters to him, risking your most precious relationships just to examine the state of his soul?

"I could name countless cases where it would obviously be better to discover someone's character through a harmless social occasion rather than paying dearly for the experience. I don't believe any Cretan, or any other person, would doubt that this is a fair test -- and a safer, cheaper, and faster one than any alternative."

"That is certainly true," Cleinias agreed.

"And this knowledge of people's natures and character will be of the greatest use in the art that manages souls. That art, if I'm not mistaken, is politics."

"Exactly so."
elegant: Many situations make us bold and shameless: anger, love, pride, wealth, beauty, and every intoxication of pleasure. Regulated wine-drinking is the safest, cheapest, and most effective way to test and train character under these conditions. Would you rather discover a savage person's nature through risky business dealings, or over a cup of wine? Would you test a man's self-control by entrusting him with your family, or by observing him at a Dionysiac festival? Knowledge gained harmlessly at the drinking table serves politics -- the art of managing human souls.
summary: The Stranger delivers the final argument: wine is the safest, cheapest, and most efficient method for testing and training character. It reveals a person's nature under the influence of boldness-inducing conditions without the real-world risks of business dealings or entrusted relationships. This knowledge of souls is the foundation of politics.
distilled: Regulated wine-drinking is the safest, cheapest, and most revealing test of character -- and knowledge of character is the foundation of politics.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. -- This is the culminating passage of Book 1. The entire argument -- from the opening question about divine lawgivers through the analysis of virtue, the puppet metaphor, and the fear-potion thought experiment -- arrives at this practical conclusion: properly supervised drinking parties serve as testing grounds for character. -- The list of boldness-inducing conditions (anger, love, pride, ignorance, avarice, cowardice, wealth, beauty, strength, pleasure) is comprehensive. -- 'The art which has the management of souls... is politics' -- this definition of politics as psychagogy (soul-management) is distinctively Platonic (cf. Gorgias 464b-465d, Phaedrus 261a-262c, Statesman 304e-305e). -- The practical comparisons (business dealings vs. drinking companions; entrusting family vs. Dionysiac observation) are vivid and persuasive. -- 'Safer, cheaper, and speedier' -- the Stranger appeals to pragmatic values his Cretan and Spartan interlocutors respect. -- Book 1 ends with the theoretical foundation laid; Book 2 will develop the connection between drinking, music, and education in detail.
